Step 7: Pallet Handling Unit Creation

A pallet handling unit is generated, and the tire handling units are packed under the pallet. This step will develop a parent child relation between Tire(HU) and Pallet(HU). Once one pallet is processed then that file will move from Inbound to archive folder.

Assumptions:

  • Each tire serial number is unique and HU is presenting the same in SAP
  • HU is also presenting Pallet in SAP.
  • Number range of both pallets are different to make this very clear to everyone.

Step 8: Warehouse Put-Away

The pallet handling unit is automatically put away into the warehouse storage bin using SAP EWM warehouse task processing.

Below is example, how stock looks like(with pallet HU & Child HU):

 

 

6.0 Error handling in S4HANA:

1)At any point of time, if someone want to check, that whether/how many XML messages received in S4HANA, which can be verified by using tcode : SXMB_MONI

As plant is working on 24 x 7then messages should come very frequently like 1-2 minutes, if it is not then need to check interface.

 

 

 

2)In case tire serial(Tire HU) number already exist then this pallet(HU) would be in error queue. Someone need to check the queue in frequent interval to avoid the errors.

3)Sometime, if XML is missing some information like stock type or quantity or some field is blank, then also pallet would be in queue error.

4) Sometime, if XML is populating wrong material number, missing some information like stock type or quantity or some field is blank, then also pallet would be in queue error.

7.0 Data Objects and Traceability:

The integration synchronizes both master and transactional data objects. This structure ensures complete traceability from production line to warehouse storage.

Master Data

  • Material master (material number of Tire type): There is mapping table for MES material number and SAP material/Product number.
    • Storage location(need to correctly align with storage type/Availability group)
    • Warehouse bins

Transactional Data

  • Handling unit (Tire serial number)
    • Handling unit (Pallet)
    • Warehouse stock

1.There is a mapping between MES stock type and SAP EWM stock type:

MES

EWM

Free      

P2(Unrestricted)

Blocked

B2(Blocked)

8.0 Reliability and Transactional Integrity:

To ensure robust system operation, the integration framework implements the following safeguards.

  • Idempotent message processing to prevent duplicate postings
    • Store-and-forward queuing in MES and Cimcorp to tolerate ERP downtime
    • Error quarantine handling for label scanning failures or quantity mismatches
    • Periodic reconciliation between MES pallet records and SAP inventory postings

These mechanisms ensure consistent synchronization between shop-floor events and enterprise inventory records

9.0 Conclusion:

This Use case, integration of a robotic tire palletizing cell with Cimcorp automation, MES, and SAP S/4HANA establishes a digitally synchronized production-to-warehouse workflow. By implementing this event-driven architecture and structured handling unit management, the system provides:

  • Real-time inventory visibility – As soon Tire is manufactured in production, with the help of palletization, we can easily check the inventory.
    • Serial-level (HU based) traceability each product - At Tire HU level Manufacturing date, expiry date, country of origin. If product is manufactured in multiple locations - At tire level, one can easily identify, where tire was manufactured. One can easily identify the expiry date of each tire, which will help to restrict to dispatch the expired tire to customer.
    • Automated warehouse execution specially in palletization - Automated warehouse execution, particularly in palletization, plays a key role in modern manufacturing and distribution environments. In such systems, finished goods coming from production lines are automatically arranged and stacked onto pallets using robotic palletizers. Sensors and conveyor systems transport the products to palletizing stations where robots organize them according to predefined stacking patterns to ensure stability and optimal space utilization.
    • Scalable automation integration - Scalable automation integration enables warehouses and manufacturing facilities to expand their automation capabilities as operational demands grow. By designing systems with modular and flexible architectures, organizations can easily add new equipment such as conveyors, robotic palletizers, without disrupting existing operations. Integration with enterprise platforms like SAP Extended Warehouse Management ensures that automated equipment can communicate seamlessly with warehouse processes, inventory management, and order fulfilment activities. This approach supports real-time data exchange, efficient material flow control, and centralized monitoring of automated systems.

entry.title: Designing Goods Receipt Strategies in SAP S/4HANA:An End-to-End Analysis of 101 and 107/109 Movement
entry.content:
Author: Naveen Chandra Sharma

(Naveen is SAP Solution architect having 29+ years of experience which includes 20+ years into SAP consulting with expertise in SAP ECC, SAP S/4HANA and integration with other external system like MES, Cimcorp etc. He worked on various type of project flavor like Implementation, Enhancement and support projects in various industries Manufacturing, Finance, Pharmaceutical, Food, Telecommunication)

1.Abstract:

 

With this paper, I want to show the major process business difference between movement type 101 Vs 107/109 in SAP S4HANA. These movement types are related to goods receipt but 101 says material is available immediately at warehouse however in 107/109, material is available once the 109 movement is executed but not during 107. However, during 107, accounting documents were created immediately, which changed the ownership immediately from vendor to customer.

As per terms, it is very clear that goods receipt is done in one step in 101 however 107/109 will have two steps to complete the goods receipt. In both cases, quality inspection will happen if quality inspection is added at material level.

Mainly this presents detailed analysis from procurement point of view wherein client is having a local vendor vs foreign vendor. Moreover, if foreign vendor is very far and takes 3 months to arrive with the customer, then we cannot ask foreign vendor for 3 months. Then in this case we will use 107/109 approach for foreign vendors.

Presenting end to end process for both the scenarios, so that everyone will get the proper guidance for designing best and compliance goods receipt process in SAP.

2.0 Introduction:

 

In current manufacturing scenarios, procurement plays very crucial role for ensuring that manufacturing is running smoothly by maintaining the better inventory control. To make the manufacturing process run, some material can be procured from multiple sources like domestic vendors and foreign vendors. Sometime, if domestic vendors are not sufficient to provide the necessary quantities, then they need to procure from foreign vendors, which is known as hybrid approach. Yes, there are complexities like, we need custom clearing agency and local vendors who can provide the logistics (from port to customer warehouse) but somehow need to accept. It is not a good decision to take ownership bit early but also need to release the supplier’s invoices but there is no other option but to proceed. Also we need to make perfect cost-effective processes, so that raw material processes are not impacting much on the cost of final product.

This presents end to end process of goods receipt process in SAP S4HANA and compares 101 vs 107/109 from Inventory availability, ownership and supplier invoicing. This gives a good picture while mapping and finalizing best procurement options.

3.0 Detailed process:

Assumptions:

#1 Material master is already created and source are already finalized

#2 Split valuation are not being used, price is same for both suppliers (Local & Foreign supplier)

#3 MRP is already executed, Purchase requisition is already created.

#4 Purchase order created with reference to Purchase requisition.

Scenario1: 107 & 109(two Steps goods receipt creation)

Movement Type 107: Goods Receipt to Valuated GR Blocked Stock

Movement type 107 is used to post goods into valuated GR blocked stock. This change the ownership of the goods is transferred to the buyer, and an accounting document is generated. However, the goods are not yet physically available in warehouse. 

Movement Type 109: Goods Receipt from Valuated GR Blocked Stock

Movement type 109 is used to transfer goods from valuated GR blocked stock to unrestricted stock. This step makes the goods available for use in warehouse inventory. 

 

#1 Purchase order creation:

Created Purchase order with “Original Acceptance”, this is the important integration point, which dictate that goods receipt strategy will have 2 steps (107 & 109).

This selection in Purchase order means, goods are accepted from an ownership / accounting perspective based on the supplier/source. It is primarily related to Ownership transfer and Logistical/financial acceptance

#2 Inbound delivery creation:

Important configuration:

1.Delivery type: ELST – It should be configured and assigned number range.

 

2.Item category: ELST – It should have movement type “109”

  1. Delivery # 180000133 created with Delivery type “ELST” and Item category “ELST:

     

 

#3 Executed 107 movement:

This step initiated when support ships the material and client will do the 107 movement using MIGO/Fiori (goods receipt). It is clearly visible that accounting document is created and ownership changes from supplier to customer. And material shows as in transit, so does not exist in physical warehouse.

#4 109 movements:

Once material physically comes to warehouse then goods receive happens using SAP Fiori (Change Inbound deliveries)

#6 Put away:  

Warehouse task is created and confirmed, finally material is in warehouse bin.

#7 Purchase order history:

Scenario2: 101(one step goods receipt)

 

#1 Purchase order creation:

Purchase order is created without “Origin Accept”, so it trigger one step goods receipt process.

#2 Inbound delivery:

Inbound delivery created with document type “EL” and item category “ELN”

#3 101 Movement:

This step was executed using SAP Fiori (Change Inbound deliveries) – Accounting document created

 

#5 Put away:

Warehouse task is created and confirmed, finally material is in warehouse bin.

 

#6 Purchase order history:

4.Conclusion:

101 scenario is applicable, when delivery time is short and after goods receipt, vendor will get ​the payment as per payment terms. In this case, when vendor sends the material as soon as ​material is received at warehouse, SAP executes the goods receipt (movement type101). ​

107/109 scenario is applicable, when delivery time is significant (more than 3 months) but vendor do not ​want to wait such long for payment. In this case, when vendor confirms that respective material is ​shipped from their side then as a step goods receipt will be completed in SAP (movement type107). ​This means material is in transit and still not received in warehouse. However, based on this ​Goods receipt (first step, Vendor payment will be made. And once material is received physically at ​warehouse then goods receipt (Second Step) will be completed (movement type 109)​.

This document helps to select the best goods receipt strategies in SAP S4HANA between 101 & 107/109.This document will also help consultants to map the process in SAP, because major configuration part is added along with end user activities. This also helps to move from 101 to 107/109 easily for same material and vice versa. This document also provides the point (Origin Accept), that decides, whether 101 approach or 109/109 approach.

entry.title: Queue Determination & Activity Areas for Internal Replenishment/Stock Transfers
entry.content:
Introduction:

Internal replenishment is an important process in SAP Extended Warehouse Management. Stock needs to be moved from bulk storage to pick face bin storage in an efficient way. However, the right resource needs to pick up the job at the right time, which is one of the common issues.

When I began to look into Queue Determination and Activity Areas to resolve this, I found that there was documentation on the topic within SAP, but there was nothing that took you through the process step by step. There was nothing within the SAP community that took all of this together.

So, I have done this configuration end-to-end in the sandbox system. This blog is all about learning how to configure Queue Determination and Activity Areas in the context of internal replenishment in Embedded EWM in S/4HANA 2025. The scenario is as follows: replenishment tasks should be assigned to the right queue so that only specific resources, in this case, forklift resources, are busy with those tasks and other processes are not disrupted

Whether you’re setting this up for the first time or trying to figure out why tasks are not routing correctly, such as bins not having Activity Areas assigned or WPTs not being marked as Queue Relevant, this guide will take you through the basics, the resources, and finally the testing.

 

Configuration Steps:

  1. Define Storage Type, Storage Bin, Activity Area Configurations and Master Data
    • Define Storage Type

Path: SPRO → IMG → SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Master Data → Define Storage Type

Define the source storage type, which's the bulk zone and the destination storage type, which is the pick face zone. If they are not already set up, you need to put them in place. The source storage type should be zone. The destination storage type should be picking face zone.

  • Define Activity Area

Path: SPRO → IMG → SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Master Data → Define Activity Areas

  • Define Storage Bin Structure

Path: SPRO → IMG → SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Master Data → Define Storage Bin Structure

  • Assign Storage Bins to Activity Areas

Path: SPRO → IMG → SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Master Data → Assign Storage Bins to Activity Areas

  • Define Sort Sequence for Activity Area

Path: SPRO → IMG → SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Master Data → Define Sort Sequence for Activity Area

  • Do Bin Sorting -- /N/SCWM/SBST


  1. Resource and Queue Management Configuration and Master Data
    • Define Resource Types

Path: SPRO → IMG → SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Cross-Process Settings → Resource Management → Define Resource Types

  • Define Queue Types

Path: SPRO → IMG → SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Cross-Process Settings → Resource Management → Define Queue Types

  • Define Queues

Path: SPRO → IMG → SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Cross-Process Settings → Resource Management → Define Queues

  • Define Queue Determination Criteria

Path: SPRO → IMG → SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Cross-Process Settings → Resource Management → Define Queue Determination Criteria

This is where you connect your Activity Area and Warehouse Process Type to a queue. The system checks these rules from top to bottom based on their priority level. It uses Activity Area and Warehouse Process Type to decide which queue to use. The order of priority matters.

  • Maintain Resource Group -- /SCWM/RGRP

  • Maintain Queue Sequence for Resource Group -- /SCWM/QSEQ

Need to assign the replenishment queue to your resource group. Then you must define the priority of the replenishment queue. The priority is important because if you have a resource group that is assigned to queues the system will look at the priority to figure out which queue to take tasks from first. This means the system will offer tasks from the replenishment queue with priority.

  • Maintain Resource -- /SCWM/RSRC

Need to open each forklift resource and assign the forklift resource to the replenishment queue. Make sure the Active flag is turned on for the  forklift resource. This is an important step that people often forget to do. If you do not do this the user will see a task screen even when the replenishment queue is full of tasks, for the forklift resource.

  • Maintain Users -- /SCWM/USER

Assign your user to the resource you created in step 2.7. This links the user login to the warehouse resource and its queue. So your user and warehouse resource are connected.

  1. Define Warehouse Process Type and Removal Strategy Configurations. 
    • Define Warehouse Process Type

Path: SPRO → IMG → SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Cross-Process Settings → Warehouse Process Types → Define Warehouse Process Types

When you are doing your WPT replenishment make sure the box that says Queue Relevant is checked. If you do not check this box the warehouse tasks will be. They will not be put into a queue for the WPT replenishment.

Also configure:

Define WPT

Define PTDI

Determine WPT

  • Specify Stock Removal Rule

Define the rule that controls how stock is picked from the source bins during restocking. The rule should specify which stock to choose from the source bins. It helps to maintain inventory levels. The rule makes sure that the right products are restocked. Source bins are used to store products before they are restocked. The rule is used during the replenishment process. Replenishment is when stock levels are brought back up to normal. Stock is selected from source bins based on this rule. It is a part of inventory management. The replenishment process relies on this rule. Source bins provide the stock that is needed. The rule helps to prevent stockouts and overstocking. Accurate stock selection is crucial, during replenishment.

  • Specify Storage Type Search Sequence

The system searches for storage types in an order when it needs to pick stock, for replenishment. It looks at the kinds of storage where the stock is kept. The system checks one type of storage after another to find the stock. It does this in a sequence to make sure it finds the stock it needs to replenish. The system searches the storage types in this order when selecting stock for replenishment.

  • Define Stock Removal Control Indicator

Controls additional stock removal behavior such as partial quantity handling.

  • Determine Storage Type Search Sequence for Stock Removal

Link the Storage Type Search Sequence to the replenishment process.

  1. Warehouse Order Creation Rule Configuration and Master Data
    • Define Number Ranges for Warehouse Order

Path: SPRO → IMG → SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Cross-Process Settings → Warehouse Order → Define Number Ranges for Warehouse Order

  • Assign Warehouse Number to Number Range Interval

  • Define Sort Rules for Warehouse Tasks

Define Sort Rule:

SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Cross-Process Settings -> Warehouse Order -> Define Sort Rules for Warehouse Tasks

Define Sort Fields:

  • Define Limit Values for the Size of a Warehouse Order

Control the maximum number of warehouse tasks or weight per warehouse order. This prevents users from receiving oversized work packets during replenishment runs

  • Define Creation Rule for Warehouse Orders

Create the Warehouse Order Creation Rule (WOCR) for replenishment. Link the sort rule, limit values, and queue assignment defined in the earlier steps.

  • Define Search Sequence of Creation Rules for Activity Areas

Testing

  • Trigger a replenishment warehouse order via /SCWM/REPL

  • Go to Warehouse Monitor -- /SCWM/MON

  • Execute /N/SCWM/REPL

  • Need to check the log

  • Press Next Task the replenishment task should appear on screen

  • Confirm the task and verify stock movement in the Warehouse Monitor

Conclusion:

Queue determination for replenishment in Embedded EWM is really complicated. It has four parts that need to work

Here are the common problems:

  • Bins are not assigned to Activity Areas
  •  The Work Pool Template or WPT is not marked as Queue Relevant
  • The resource is not set to Active in the queue assignment

When all four parts of Queue Determination for replenishment are set up correctly the system automatically sends replenishment tasks to the correct resource. This makes your warehouse run smoothly and it is easier to manage Queue Determination for replenishment. Queue Determination for replenishment in Embedded EWM is very important. I hope this helps you with Queue Determination for replenishment. Please leave a comment about Queue Determination for replenishment. Share with others about Queue Determination, for replenishment.

entry.title: Mastering Resource Management in SAP EWM
entry.content:
Behind every smooth warehouse operation in SAP EWM, resource management plays a huge role.
It ensures work is assigned intelligently, executed efficiently, and aligned with real business priorities.

Here, I want to show you something different - how the system actually thinks. How does it make decisions? How it behaves under the hood when resources are being managed. We're not starting from the surface; we're starting from the bottom and building our way up.

By the end of this, you won't just know what to configure - you'll understand why the system behaves the way it does.

Managing warehouse resources in high-volume automotive spare parts distribution centers requires tight synchronization between configuration and execution.

Here’s a quick cheat sheet on key T-Codes and the business logic behind them:

Key Configuration Codes:

Queue Determination → /SCWM/QSEQ

Warehouse Tasks are auto-sorted into specialized work streams by WPT, Activity Area & Storage Type.

→ Engines ≠ Small Parts. The system knows that before the operator does.

Resource Definition → /SCWM/RSRC

Every forklift, picker, and reach truck is defined here, with its resource type and capacity constraints.

→ No resource = no assignment. Garbage in, garbage out.

Resource Group Assignment → /SCWM/RGRP

Links resources to eligible queues. Only qualified operators get assigned to the right task class.

→ You don't send a cherry picker to handle a pallet of axles.

 

Queue Priority Sequence → /SCWM/QSEQ

Controls task urgency logic. Production supply & urgent dealer orders surface to the top — automatically.

→ No manual intervention. Priority is governed, not guessed.

Task Interleaving → /SCWM/QTSQ

The real efficiency multiplier. Combines Put away → Picking → Replenishment in one optimized travel loop.

→ Eliminates deadheading (empty travel). Operators stay productive across the full shift.

 RF Execution → /SCWM/RFUI

Guided, Poka-Yoke scanning at the point of execution. Zero tolerance for mispicks or wrong put-away.

→ The system validates. The operator confirms. Accuracy is non-negotiable.

 

The Workflow (Real Scenario):

The Workflow (Real Scenario):

In Warehouse 3900, inbound Small Automotive Parts (fast-moving SKUs) are received, and Warehouse Tasks are created based on source and destination data.

  • The system assigns Queues based on activity area, storage type, and process type [V_T346L → WP 1010 | PTWY → INBOUND]
  • Now the system looks for a Warehouse Order. The WOCR assigns it to an Activity Area — that Activity Area, mapped in QSEQ, triggers the queue assignment to the resource [/SCWM/QSEQ → HUMA | Seq 1 → INBOUND]
  • When operator MOHD logs into RFUI, the Resource Group logic dynamically assigns tasks [/SCWM/RSRC → MOHD | RT01 | HUMA | YE00]
  • High-priority tasks (e.g., production supply or urgent dealer orders) are pushed first [QSEQ Seq 1 governs the order, not the supervisor]
  • Task Interleaving ensures operators perform Put away → Picking → Replenishment in one optimized travel loop [/SCWM/QTSQ → HUMA | Seq 1 → OUTB]
  • Operator completes the Task and logs off the resource.

Result: Reduced empty travel, faster order fulfillment, and improved labor utilization

Conclusion:

Resource management in SAP EWM isn't a configuration exercise; it's a design decision. Queues reflect real work streams. Resource groups reflect real skill sets. Task interleaving reflects real movement on the floor. When you understand that, you stop configuring the system and start designing it. In automotive distribution, the stakes are real. A delayed Automotive parts kit doesn't sit in a bin. it stops a production line. Get the foundation right, and the warehouse runs itself. That's not an exaggeration; that's just good configuration in practice.

entry.title: Physical Inventory through WM in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud – Real-Time Implementation
entry.content:
Physical Inventory through WM in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

In one of our recent projects, I worked on implementing the Warehouse Management process in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud. As part of this, I was responsible for configuring and testing the Physical Inventory (PI) process through WM. As part of this, I configured and executed the Physical Inventory process through WM. The complete PI cycle was tested end-to-end using the standard SAP process, ensuring that the system stock accurately reflects the actual physical stock in the warehouse.

Below, we will walk through the configuration setup and the practical execution of the process.

Step 1: Activity Area Configuration

Menu Path:

Manage Your Solution -> Configuration -> Logistics, Transportation and Warehousing -> Warehouse Management -> Warehouse Master Data -> Activity Area

Here, I used the standard Activity Area “INVE”, and the Warehouse Process Category is maintained as “4 – PI”.

Define Activity Area: I created new Activity Area and assigned to the respective warehouse.

Assign Storage Bins to Activity Areas: In this step, the created Activity Area is assigned to the respective Storage Type. By doing this, all the storage bins under that storage type are linked to the Activity Area, which allows the system to process Physical Inventory activities for those bins.

Define Sort Sequence for Activity Areas: In this step, I assigned the Activity Area along with the relevant Activity and Storage Type. This configuration helps the system decide the sequence in which storage bins are picked during the Physical Inventory process, so that the counting is carried out in an organized way.

Step 2: Define Physical Inventory Area

Menu Path:

Warehouse Management -> Internal Warehouse Processes -> Physical Inventory in the Warehouse

Define Physical Inventory Area: In this step, I defined the Physical Inventory Area by assigning the Warehouse Number along with the relevant Activity Area. Additionally, the required Physical Inventory process indicators are maintained, which control how the counting and posting process is executed within the warehouse.

Define Number Range for Physical Inventory Documents: The number range for Physical Inventory documents is maintained.

Specify Physical-Inventory-Specific Settings in the Warehouse: In this step, the Physical Inventory specific settings are maintained at the warehouse level.

Key details such as the Fiscal Year Variant and Number Range for PI documents are configured based on business requirements.

Define Tolerance Group for Difference Analyzer: In this step, I defined the Tolerance Group and assigned it to the Warehouse. The tolerance limits are maintained based on business requirements, which control how inventory differences are handled during the Physical Inventory process.

For testing purposes, I have set it as “No Tolerance”, so that the system considers the full difference without any limits.

Define Tolerance Group for Posting Differences: I defined the Tolerance Group for Posting Differences.

If the difference exceeds the maintained tolerance, the system can restrict or control the posting process.

For testing purposes, the “No Tolerance” option is maintained, so all differences are considered without any restriction.

As shown above, the system throws an error stating that the document item cannot be posted due to exceeded tolerances.

This behavior is controlled by the tolerance settings maintained in the configuration. If the difference goes beyond the defined limit, the system restricts the posting of the Physical Inventory document. This helps ensure proper validation and control over inventory adjustments.

Define Tolerance Group for Recounting: I defined the Tolerance Group for Recounting.

For testing purposes, the “No Tolerance” option is maintained, so recount is not restricted by any limits.

Assign Tolerance Group - Count Confirmation App: I mapped the Tolerance Group to the Warehouse User (Warehouse Person).

Assign Tolerance Group- Differences App:

Define Priorities: Standard configuration is used, where priority levels like Very High, High, Medium, and Low are already defined.

Define Standard Reason for Each Phy. Inv. Procedure: Each Physical Inventory procedure (such as Annual, Cycle Counting, or Ad-hoc) is linked with a specific reason code. This helps in identifying the purpose of the Physical Inventory process and improves tracking and reporting, I maintained STD.

Define Print Form for Physical Inventory Documents: The required fields to be printed on the Physical Inventory document are selected based on business needs. This ensures that the printed document contains all necessary details for warehouse users during the counting process.

Step 3: Output Parameter Determination

Ensure that the Warehouse Number is correctly assigned to the respective WM Physical Inventory process in the output determination. The appropriate print queue (printer settings) should also be maintained so that Physical Inventory documents can be printed as required.

Step 4: Define Search Sequence of Creation Rules for Activity Areas:

Menu path: Warehouse Management-> Warehouse Cross Processes-> Warehouse Order-> Define Search Sequence of Creation Rules for Activity Areas

In this step, I assigned the Warehouse Number, Activity Area, and Activity along with the Warehouse Order creation rule. I maintained the standard WO creation rule (SR04 – WO for Physical Inventory without limit) to ensure proper grouping of warehouse tasks during the Physical Inventory process.

 

Testing the Physical Inventory Process

Now, we proceed to test the Physical Inventory process flow for product-specific (AS) using the standard WM process. There are several Physical Inventory counting procedures available, and for testing purposes, I am using “AS – Annual Physical Inventory (Product-Specific)”

Step 1: Create Physical Inventory Document

App: Create Inventory Documents – Physical Inventory

The Physical Inventory document is created for a product-specific “AS” scenario.

Here, I selected the product “FG Material” with batch “0000000454” for the Physical Inventory count.

Before performing the Physical Inventory count, I verified the current stock available in the warehouse.

Verified at the IM stock level.

I selected the required material “FG Material” along with batch “0000000454” and created the Physical Inventory document using the Create Inventory Documents – Physical Inventory app.

Step 2: Count Physical Inventory - Paper-Driven Counting

Once the PI document is created, the system automatically generates a Warehouse Order (WO) in the background for the counting process.

In a paper-driven process, the PI document can be printed using this app and provided to the warehouse person for physical counting.

Step 3: Counting (Enter Physical Count)

Once the warehouse person completes the physical counting based on the printed document, the count needs to be entered into the system using the same app.

Here, the system stock was 100 Qty. For testing, I reduced 1 quantity from the respective Handling Unit (301742) to simulate a difference scenario. After entering the updated count, I confirmed the count in the system, which resulted in a stock difference.

Step 4: Post the Physical Inventory Document

After confirming the count, I proceeded to post the Physical Inventory document. I selected the PI document and executed “Post Physical Inventory Document”.

Since I reduced the count by 1 quantity, once the Physical Inventory document was posted, the system automatically identified the stock difference.

The difference can be posted, which updates the stock and completes the adjustment process in the system.

After posting the Physical Inventory differences, the stock can be verified again at both WM and IM levels. As shown, the stock is now reduced from 100 to 99, reflecting the Physical Inventory adjustment.

IM Stock level

Conclusion:

From this implementation, I have shown the Physical Inventory configuration through WM and the end-to-end PI process execution for product-based “AS” in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud. This helps ensure accurate stock tracking and better control over inventory differences in the warehouse.

entry.title: SAP EWM Storage Control Deep Dive: Combining Process-Oriented and Layout-Oriented Strategies
entry.content:
SAP EWM Storage Control Deep Dive: Combining Process-Oriented and Layout-Oriented Strategies

Author: Naveen Chandra Sharma

(Naveen is SAP Solution architect having 29+ years of experience which includes 20+ years into SAP consulting with expertise in SAP ECC, SAP S/4HANA and integration with other external system like MES, CIMCORP etc. He worked on various type of project flavor like Implementation, Enhancement and support projects in various industries Manufacturing, Finance, Pharmaceutical, Food, Telecommunication)

1.Abstract:

This use case considers a scenario wherein both storage control processes are required at same time. In this example, two processes (Unloading + Put away) and before final put away it should through Intermediate bin.

Here Process oriented storage control is having

 #1 Unloading

#2 Put away

However, during #2, Layout oriented storage control triggers depending upon the final storage bin and move the material to Intermediate bin and the complete the put away process.

                                                                                                                           

 

2.0 Introduction:

One of the important feature of SAP EWM is Storage control process.  It is mechanism which dictate how material will move inside the warehouse with how many steps and what would be the sequence of those steps. It says stage like sequence of movement and other steps like staging, deconsolidation, value addition services and finally physical Movement among the storage types. There are two storage type control:

  1. Process oriented storage control(POSC)

This control the logical process steps like Unloading, Deconsolidation, Labelling, Quality inspection, Packing, Unpacking, staging, put away etc.

  1. Layout oriented storage control(LOSC)

This control the physical movement of material inside the warehouse, like staging area, Conveyor routing, Intermediate bins etc.

3.0 Detailed process:

As per the business requirement, we can use POSC and LOSC separately or can even make hybrid approach, which combine POSC and LOSC both.

In this use case, we are using the hybrid approach POSC + LOSC for Inbound process. POSC is having two steps Unload and Put away, however during Put away, before final put away, need to move the material at intermediate bin and finally complete the put away.

#1 Door Determination:

Configuration and master data settings are as follows:

  1. Configuration

 

 

 

 

  1. b) Master data

b1) Created below bins as staging area under storage type 9010

 

b2) Created below intermediate bin under intermediate storage type 0082

 

b3) Setting Warehouse process type along with staging area group, bay and warehouse door.

 

#2 POSC and LOSC:

  1. Configuration

 

Final storage type for raw material

 

 

Intermediate Storage type:

 

Warehouse process type:

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

POSC:

 

 

 

LOSC configuration:

 

Testing:

#1) Product Master(/SCWM/MAT1):

 

 

#2 Inbound delivery(VL31N):

 

 

 

#3 Inbound delivery distributed in EWM(/SCWM/PRDI):

 

 

Step1: Below WT/WO is created(/SCWM/MON): Confirmed WT # 1712 in background, it means unloading process is completed

#1As pruduct master is having process determination as T8, warehouse process WP10 determined.

#2 Final storage type ST05 is determined, based on storage search configuration.

#3 As Product master is having door determination D001, it will determined the goods movement bin.

 

 

 

Step2: AS soon as unloading process is completed and destination storage type is ST15, so LOSC triggered and moved the stock to Intermediate bin 0821-IN-BIN

 

Step3: Finally, material will be moved from Intermediate bin 0821-IN-BIN to final bin ST15-05

 

 

 

 

 

4.Conclusion:

This scenario is applied when POSC and LOSC needs to be executed combinedly. The combined use of POSC and LOSC in SAP EWM enables organizations to accurately model both process-driven steps and physical warehouse movements within a single integrated framework.

The configuration, master data, and transactional flow outlined in this document provide a good reference for fellow SAP consultants to design, map, and implement robust storage control scenarios in SAP EWM.

entry.title: Intracompany STO with inbound ASR in Embedded EWM –Automatic IBD Creation at PGI With FU Reassign.
entry.content:
Overview 

This blog describes an intracompany stock transport order (STO) scenario using Advanced shipping and receiving (ASR) for inbound execution with embedded SAP EWM. 

A key enhancement in this scenario is the automatic creation of the inbound delivery at post goods issue (PGI) of the outbound delivery, triggered via SPED output, along with automatic reassignment of the same Freight Unit (FU) from outbound to inbound delivery. 

The solution supports end–to-end execution across SAP S/4 HANA, SAP TM, SAP EWM, and ASR, ensuring uninterrupted transportation and warehouse execution using a single Freight Unit and Freight Order. 

Business Scope and Supported Scenarios 

This functionality applies to intracompany stock transfers and supports inbound ASR execution when outbound processing is performed from either EWM-managed or non-EWM storage locations. 

Supported Combinations 

  • Outbound ASR shipping point+EWM storage location -->Inbound ASR shipping point+ EWM Storage location 
  • Outbound ASR shipping point+EWM storage location -->Inbound ASR+non-EWM Storage location 
  • Outbound ASR shipping point+ NON EWM storage location -->Inbound ASR+EWM Storage location 
  • Outbound non-ASR +NON EWM storage location -->Inbound ASR+EWM Storage location 

 This flexibility allows organizations to run Hybrid Landscapes without breaking ASR or transportation execution. 

Key Features

  • Advanced Shipping and Receiving (ASR) enabled for inbound STO processing 
  • Embedded EWM support for inbound warehouse execution 
  • Outbound execution from EWM or non-EWM storage locations 
  • Automatic inbound delivery creation at PGI using SPED output 
  • Automatic reassignment of the same Freight Unit from outbound to inbound delivery 
  • Continuous TM execution using one Freight Unit and one Freight order 
  • Updated document flow in the TM Freight Order after reassignment 

End-to-End Process Flow 

1.Transportation and Delivery Preparation

  • Stock Transport Order (STO) is created with EWM relevance. ￼

 

  • Freight Unit (FU) is created in SAPTM. 

 

           

 

  • Plan Freight Unit into FO using TM Optimizer, Automatic carrier selection is performed. 

 

          

 

         

 

  • Create Outbound Delivery (OBD) with FO.

 

          

 

  • Delivery proposal is generated and distributed to SAPEWM 

     

2.Outbound Warehouse Execution (Source Warehouse)

  • Picking and packing are executed in SAP EWM.
  •  

  •  

     

  • ASR execution at the source warehouse: 
  • Check-in 
  • Door check-in 
  • Loading of packages 
  • Loading end  
  • Delivery split is performed, if required 
  • ASR cockpit checkout and PGI (Post Goods Issue) are executed.
  •  
  •  

     

3.SPED Output and Inbound Delivery Creation (New Feature), inbound warehouse execution 

  • SPED output is triggered automatically at PGI of the outbound delivery.
  •  

           

 

  • Inbound delivery (IBD) is created automatically
  •  
  •  

     

  • Freight Unit reassignment occurs automatically:
  • The same FU used for outbound execution is reassigned to the inbound delivery 
  • No new FU created 
  •  

  •  

  • This step is the core enhancement and ensures FU continuity across outbound and inbound execution. 
  • ASR at destination warehouse 
  • Check-in 
  • Door check-in 
  • Unloading of packages 
  • Unloading end  
  • Post Goods Receipt (PGR) for inbound delivery
  •  
  •  

     

  • ASR check out
  • Put Away to destination bin in EWM
  •  

  •  

Benefits 

  • Increased flexibility for ASR processes- supports multiple combinations of EWM and non-EWM storage locations. 
  • Improved Intracompany Stock Movement- Ensures a smooth, integrated STO execution across outbound and inbound logistics. 
  • Reduced Manul Effort- Automation of inbound delivery creation and FU reassignment eliminates manual steps. 
  • Increased Data Accuracy- single FU and FO ensures consistent transportation and warehouse data 

Conclusion

  • The enhanced intracompany STO with inbound ASR in embedded EWM delivers a seamless, end-to-end logistics process by tightly integrating SAP TM, SAP EWM and ASR. 
  • The automatic creation of inbound delivery at PGI via SPED output, combined with Freight unit reassignment, ensures continuous transportation visibility, eliminates document fragmentation, and significantly reduces operational effort. 
  • For organizations operating hybrid EWM and non-EWM landscapes, this functionality provides execution flexibility, better data consistency, and a simplified logistics process.

entry.title: Mastering Inbound Execution in SAP EWM: Integrating WOCR with POSC, LOSC with Door determination
entry.content:
Author: Naveen Chandra Sharma

(Naveen is SAP Solution architect having 29+ years of experience which includes 20+ years into SAP consulting with expertise in SAP ECC, SAP S/4HANA and integration with other external system like MES, CIMCORP etc. He worked on various type of project flavor like Implementation, Enhancement and support projects in various industries Manufacturing, Finance, Pharmaceutical, Food, Telecommunication)

1.Abstract:

This paper describes the scenario wherein warehouse is receiving the raw material from Vendor and process flow is as follows:

#1 Enter truck through warehouse door -> Unload the material in staging area ->Put away

#2 Put away is not possible but through Intermediate Bin

#3 Need to group warehouse tasks

Seeing the above requirement, we need to design the Door determination, POSC (process-oriented storage control), LOSC (Layout oriented storage control) and WOCR (warehouse order creation rule). In this blog, I have designed said process, which will help fellow consultants to understand the business requirement and end to end design process.

a) Door Determination: System automatically decides which unloading door, a truck should go to when goods arrive.

b) WOCR (Warehouse order creation rule): This rule, group the warehouse tasks into a warehouse order, which is the efficient way to execute the warehouse order by a warehouse person. This will help to improve the productivity of warehouse person keeping the similar warehouse tasks (area, activity, quantity etc.) in respective warehouse order. This also tells, how many warehouse tasks will be in one warehouse order.

c) POSC  (Process oriented storage control process): This is one of the storage control types, based on process type. This defines; how many steps will be there for Inbound/outbound processes.  Example of steps in inbound/outbound process is as follows:

c1) Inbound : Unloading -> Deconsolidation -> Putaway

c2) Outbound: Picking-> Packing->Staging

d) LOSC (Layout oriented storage control process): This is one of the storage control types, based on path. It defines the route (physical path) goods should take inside warehouse base of designed layout. This is applicable, when direct movement is not possible or warehouse has conveyors or automated warehouses like ASRS/MFS.

WOCR finalize how warehouse task (WT) sorted and grouped into warehouse orders (WO). This applies when WT are created and need to define that how many WT will be under warehouse orders. This will help, what would be total resource workload and how it will be assigned to resources. This improves warehouse efficiency by grouping tasks logically into executable units, reducing travel time and optimizing resource usage. WOCR play a crucial role in optimizing inbound execution by intelligently grouping warehouse tasks into executable units (Warehouse Orders).

WOCR enhances inbound warehouse operations by grouping tasks intelligently, reducing travel time, improving resource utilization, and ensuring efficient execution of put away and handling processes in SAP EWM.

This title, consider a scenario wherein Inbound process needs WOCR along with POSC + LOSC. Normally POSC triggers from Warehouse process type (WPT) and following by LOSC and top of that WOCR will be applied. This use case considers a scenario wherein both storage control processes are required at same time. In this example, POSC will have two processes (Unloading + Put away) and during put way only LOSC will be applied (move material to intermediate bin), top of that WOCR will be applied on all Warehouse order creation.

Here Process oriented storage control is having below two steps:

#1 Unloading

#2 Put away

However, during step #2, Layout oriented storage control triggers (intermediate bin) depending upon the final storage bin and move the material to Intermediate bin and then complete the put away process.

2.0 Introduction:

Here, we are having business scenario for Inbound raw materials. This process will start from Door determination, unloading the material at staging area and finally put away, however direct put away is not possible because of Intermediate bin. Technically process will start from door determination, POSC, LOSC and top of that WOCR will be implemented.

Need to make the needed customization, master data and finally end to end process.

Door determination

System automatically decides which unloading door, a truck should go to when goods arrive. 

Warehouse order creation rule(WOCR)

This control to sort and group the WT task and also finalize that that how many WT will be under one warehouse order

Process oriented storage control(POSC)

This control the logical process steps like Unloading, Deconsolidation, Labelling, Quality inspection, Packing, Unpacking, staging, put away etc.

Layout oriented storage control(LOSC)

This control the physical movement of material inside the warehouse, like staging area, Conveyor routing, Intermediate bins etc.

3.0 Detailed process:

As per the business requirement, Door determination will be made, follows by POSC, which will be triggered from WPT.  Next LOSC will be triggered during put away (step from POSC). WOCR will be applied on all warehouse order. In this paper, using WOCR + POSC + LOSC, for Inbound process.

#1 Configuration of WOCR:

Filter: This is required to identify the weight/volume of warehouse task at item/subtotal level.

FLT1 is created for Item level weight less than 10 Kg.

FLT2 is created for Item level weight is more than 10KG and less than 100Kg.

Limit: This defines majorly how many WT per WO.

LTM1 is created one warehouse task per warehouse order.

LTM1 is created two warehouse task per warehouse order.

WOCR: Rule can be defined majorly by WO creation category, Filter, Limit and packing profile

WOC1 was created with FLT1 and LMT1:

WOC2 was created with FLT2 and LMT1

WOC3 was created with FLT1 and LMT2:

WOC4 was created with FLT2 and LMT2:

Search Sequence: This will help to search the respective Warehouse order, as per the conditions

#2 Door Determination:

Configuration and master data settings are as follows:

Configuration

Made three staging area, because we are assuming there are three doors for in-warding the raw material.

Made below three Door determination groups:

Made three doors (DOR1/DOR2/DOR3) and link with door determination group.

Assigned staging area to Door.

b) Master data

b1) Created below bins as staging area under storage type 9010

 

b2) Created below intermediate bin under intermediate storage type 0082

 

b3) Setting Warehouse process type along with staging area group, bay and warehouse door.

#3 POSC and LOSC:

Configuration

Final storage type (ST03) for raw material

Intermediate Storage type (082): This is HU required Storage type

Warehouse process type: 

Created 3030 for LOSC

Created WPT as WP10 for POSC triggering 

Define Control indicator T8 for WPT # WPT10 determination:

POSC: Below external steps (17PT - Put away & 17UN-Unloading) created

Made POSC as "POS5" and assigned two steps 17UN and 17PT -Automatic created WT 

LOSC configuration:

#4 Testing

#1) Product Master(/SCWM/MAT1): Created material # MAT-1030 with process type determination as T8 and storage group determination as D001(This will determine door as DOR1)

#2 Inbound delivery (VL31N): 180107761 was created

#3 Inbound delivery distributed in EWM(/SCWM/PRDI): 180107761

Step1: Below WT/WO is created(/SCWM/MON)

#1As product master is having process determination as T8, warehouse process WP10 determined.

#2 As per WOCR, respective WOCR # WOC1 was populated.

WOC1 was triggered because line-item level weight was 5 Kg, so filter FLT1 was triggered and it will create one warehouse per one warehouse order as per LMT1. In this case there was only

#3 Product master is having door determination D001, it determined the goods movement bin.

Step2: AS soon as unloading process is completed and destination storage type is ST16, so LOSC triggered and one WO # 1415 was created to move the material to Intermediate bin. And WOC1 was also populated. Next WT # 1862 is under hold.

Step3: As soon as WO # 1415 confirmed, WT # 1862 becomes active.

Step4: After WT # 1862 confirmed material moved to final bin ST16-08

#5 Conclusion

This scenario demonstrates how POSC and LOSC can be effectively combined with WOCR to manage both process-driven steps and physical movement within a unified inbound flow. While WOCR is not always emphasized in standard inbound scenarios, its use adds significant value by optimizing warehouse task grouping and execution efficiency. Integrating WOCR with POSC and LOSC enables a more structured, scalable, and operationally efficient inbound process aligned with real-world warehouse requirements.

In nutshell this blog will help to fellow consultants for simulating below business scenario:

#1 There was more than one door of various sizes, and some materials are huge in volume, so with door determination process, material will get the correct door. 

#2 Business needs two steps (Unload and put away in a sequence) for raw material inbound process then it is achievable with POSC.

#3 Put away is not possible with final bin, then Intermediate bin is required, here LOSC approach is very much required.

#4 Need to segregate the warehouse order based of item weight then can be achieved by WOCR.

Challenges/important points:

  • Most important, HU is very much required to initiate this process (packing is very much required)
  • POSC does not trigger because either bin is not available or Bins are not sorted.
  • LOSC does not trigger, if correct activity area is no updated in LOSC
  • WOCR will not trigger, if correct weight is not maintained at material master level

entry.title: Implementing Labor Management in SAP EWM: From Configuration to Reporting
entry.content:
H1.0: Abstract & Introduction:

Labor Management in SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) enables organizations to define, determined and increase the workforce performance in overall warehouse operations. This blog populates end-to-end design, customization, master data setup and Testing steps required to setup the Labor Management. This encompass both direct task and Indirect tasks, how they are setup, processed and analyzed using activity area, resources and ELS (engineered labor resources. This document works as bible to design and implement labor management process in a complex warehouse.

Example of executed workload for a specific day for multiple resources: 

As per my prior experience, with below reasons, labor management implementations fail in practice:

  • Engineered Labor Standards (ELS) – The planned data was not practical or accurate, as it was copied from other sites instead of being based on real operations.
  • Warehouse Team not aligned – Warehouse persons were not taken into confidence before implementing Labor Management.
  • Incorrect KPIs – Only speed (planned time) was considered, while other important parameters such as productivity and quality were ignored.
  • Perception of punishment – Warehouse personnel believed the system would be used by top management to punish them 

Note: Outbound process will be designed same way as Inbound process(mentioned in this blog)

H2.0: Detailed process:

This blog is made for below business scenario:

  • There will be Direct tasks (WO) and Indirect tasks (Meeting, maintenance activities etc.) both.
  • Direct tasks need determined for Put away external steps (staging area 9010)
  • Performance needs to be determined for Direct task aggregate for a day
  • Workload needs to be triggered, if WO weight is less than 50 KG or more than 50 KG.
  • Planned duration would be fixed for less than 50 KG and if it is more then, planned duration time would the doubled.

H2.1: Customization:

H2.1.1: Activate Labor Management + Activate Internal process steps

Under this step, labor management needs to be activated at warehouse level, which is one of the first and mandatory step.

This step activates Labor Management for internal warehouse processes by assigning relevant process steps (e.g., PICK, PUT, PAC, INDL). Each step is enabled for performance tracking, and the workload for RF warehouse orders is defined to ensure accurate labor measurement and reporting.

* INDL is internal process step for Indirect labor task.

H2.1.2: Define External Process steps: This configuration maps external storage process steps to internal process steps used in Labor Management. Indirect activities such as meetings and maintenance are linked to the internal step INDL, ensuring proper tracking of non-operational work.

 And I have seen, this configuration is sometimes missed by many consultants. This activity must be defined properly and assigned to external steps for indirect activities such as meetings and maintenance.

H2.1.3: Define Activity: Warehouse activities are defined and categorized according to process types such as stock removal, put away, internal movements, and physical inventory. These activity definitions are critical for assigning engineered labor standards (ELS) and enabling accurate labor tracking.

H2.1.4: Assigned Plan activity area to Storage types: In this configuration, planning activity areas are assigned to specific storage types. This mapping is essential for Labor Management, as it allows the system to calculate workload, assign resources, and evaluate performance based on defined operational zones.

H2.1.5: Activation of ODM Components for Indirect Labor Tasks: ODM is a framework that enables for tracking and processing for order related data for labor management and EWL (Executed workload). Order document types are WO, WT, Internal documents and Dependent objects are Activities, resource and execution Data. Order data area is really logical grouping of data, which is used for Labor management and EWL processing.  

H2.1.6: Determine the number range for Indirect labor: Number range defined for Indirect labor task.

H2.1.7:Number range interval needs to be created for assignment of number range.

H2.1.8: Determined engineered labor standard:

Direct ELS values are maintained for warehouse process steps by defining conditions, constants, time units, and formulas. These settings are critical for calculating standard execution times and enabling accurate labor performance measurement. For each step, there are three records:

Record #1: External step 17PUN, planned time is 7 MIN

Record#2: if CON1 is satisfied (means if workload weight is <50 KG) then planned time would be 7 MIN

Record#3: If CON2 is satisfied(means if workload weight is >50 KG) then planned time would be 2x7=14 MIN

H2.1.9 : Conditions for Engineered Labor Standards: /N/SCWM/LM_CE

H2.1.9.1 :Condition CON1 is created, if workload weight is < 50 KG

H2.1.9.2 : Condition CON2 is created, if workload weight is > 50 KG

* will be used in above to change the mode as expert mode and then only you can add/change the values. And Field name can be picked from #1 and operator from #2 and Number/constant from #3.

H2.1.9.2 Formula for Engineered Labor Standards: /N/SCWM/LM_FE(entering the formula also, will have same process as above mentioned for conditions)

H2.1.9.2.1 Created FOR1, if the weight is < 50 then planned time would be 7 MIN(defined at record #1)

H2.1.9.2.2 Created FOR2, if the weight is > 50 then planned time would be 14 MIN ( 2 X defined at record#1)

H2.1.10 : Activate Change Documents in Labor Management

In this step, specific Labor Management activities are selected for change document activation. This ensures that any updates to processor data, performance documents, and time and attendance are recorded for audit and traceability purposes.

H2.1.10.1: Activate change Documents for processor

By activating this option, the system records all changes made to processor-related data. This improves transparency and supports auditing and troubleshooting within Labor Management processes.

H2.1.11 : Processor: In this configuration, processor types, reporting groups, and work categories are defined to structure labor resources. These elements are critical for capturing labor activities, grouping performance data, and enabling accurate reporting in Labor Management. Proper configuration also allows differentiation between workforce categories, supports KPI-based performance evaluation, and enhances visibility into operational efficiency across warehouse functions.

H2.1.11.1 Define Processor type:

This configuration is essential for reporting purposes, as it defines how labor resources are categorized and analyzed within the system. In this step, processor types are defined to distinguish between different categories of labor, such as internal employees and external (contract) workers.

This classification plays a critical role in accurate labor tracking, as it enables the system to capture and report activities based on workforce type. It also supports detailed performance analysis, allowing businesses to compare productivity across different labor groups.

Furthermore, processor type differentiation is important for cost analysis, as internal and external resources often have different cost structures. By maintaining this configuration correctly, organizations can achieve better visibility into workforce performance, optimize resource utilization, and make more informed operational decisions.

H2.1.11.2 Define Reporting Groups for Processors: 

Under this step, processors are grouped into reporting categories (e.g., inbound and outbound). These groups are used to consolidate labor data and support detailed performance reporting and analysis within SAP EWM Labor Management.

By organizing processors into logical reporting groups, the system enables aggregation of labor activities at a functional level, allowing businesses to analyze productivity across different warehouse operations. This helps in identifying bottlenecks, comparing performance between inbound and outbound processes, and improving operational efficiency.

Additionally, reporting groups provide a structured foundation for KPI analysis, enabling managers to monitor workload distribution, resource utilization, and process efficiency. This classification also supports better decision-making by offering clear visibility into how labor resources are performing across different operational areas.

H2.1.11.3 Define Work Categories: 

This configuration is also used for reporting purposes, where work categories are defined to classify different types of labor activities. In this scenario, three work categories are maintained—such as floor operations, back office, and support—to clearly distinguish between operational and non-operational tasks.

This classification helps in separating direct and indirect labor, enabling more accurate performance tracking and reporting. It also allows businesses to analyze productivity based on the nature of work and improve resource allocation across different functional areas.

H2.1.12 Shift Management: 

Shift management is used to define and manage workforce schedules within the warehouse. In this step, shift timings, break schedules, and capacity variants are configured to reflect actual working conditions. This enables the system to accurately determine labor availability, calculate utilization rates, and evaluate performance.

Proper shift configuration ensures that labor activities are measured within the correct time framework, improving the accuracy of KPIs and workforce planning. It also helps in aligning resource allocation with operational demand, thereby enhancing overall warehouse efficiency.

H2.1.12.1 Define Capacity Variants: 

Capacity variants are configured to represent different levels of workforce availability, such as minimum and normal capacity. This setup allows the system to plan and allocate resources dynamically based on changing operational demands.

By using capacity variants, organizations can handle scenarios such as reduced staffing, peak workload periods, or standard operating conditions. This flexibility improves workforce utilization, supports efficient resource planning, and ensures better alignment between labor availability and warehouse activities.

H2.1.12.2 Maintain Shift Management Defaults:

In this step, default values for shift management are defined, including resource capacity variants and applicable working days. These defaults provide a baseline for workforce scheduling and ensure consistency across planning activities.

By maintaining these settings, the system can automatically determine labor availability, support accurate scheduling, and align workforce capacity with operational requirements. This helps improve planning efficiency, reduces manual effort, and ensures reliable performance calculations.

H2.2:  Master data:

H2.2.1: Create a Processor - T Code: /N/SCMB/PRR1

In this step, a Business Partner (e.g., #50000283) is created and assigned the role of a warehouse worker (LM0001). This setup is essential for representing labor resources within SAP EWM Labor Management.

Key attributes such as labor factor, supply chain unit, and warehouse assignment are maintained to ensure that labor activities are captured accurately. These attributes play a critical role in calculating workload, measuring performance, and enabling detailed reporting.

By correctly configuring the processor, the system can track individual worker performance, support resource planning, and provide meaningful insights into workforce productivity.

This is one of the main master data, so without this none of labor management activities will work at all.

H2.2.2: Assign User to BP created - /SCWM/MON

In this step, the SAP user is assigned to the Business Partner representing the warehouse worker. This assignment is essential to link system user activities with the corresponding processor in SAP EWM Labor Management.

By establishing this connection, the system can accurately capture labor activities performed by the user and associate them with the correct processor. It also enables users to analyze processor-level data, including workforce classification, reporting group, and system assignment.

This master data plays a key role in monitoring labor execution, ensuring data accuracy, and supporting detailed performance evaluation and reporting.

H2.2.3 Maintain Shifts: /SCWM/SHIFT

In this master data step, warehouse shifts are configured by defining start and end times, break durations, and shift factors. These parameters form the foundation for workforce scheduling and are critical for accurate labor tracking and performance measurement.

For example, a shift (e.g., 1017_A) is created with clearly defined working hours. This ensures that all labor activities are recorded within the correct time frame and aligned with actual operational schedules.

These settings are essential for accurate labor planning, time tracking, and performance evaluation in Labor Management. Maintained the shift name as 1017_A with starting time and end timings. 

H2.2.4 : Maintain processor work schedule: /SCWM/SHIFT_WS

In this step, work schedules are assigned to processors by defining valid date ranges and corresponding shift patterns. This configuration ensures that each warehouse worker’s availability is accurately maintained in the system.

For example, a work schedule is created for processor 50000283 for the next six months, aligning the worker with predefined shifts. This allows the system to:

  • Accurately determine workforce availability
  • Calculate utilization and productivity metrics
  • Ensure labor activities are recorded within valid working periods

 

H2.3: Test Scenarios:

H2.3.1: Scenario 1: Weight is less than 50 KG

H2.3.1.1:  Inbound delivery 180107832(Created with tcode VL31N) with Handling unit(pallet)

H2.3.1.2: Warehouse order 1445(created using /SCWM/PRDI)

1: Plan Activity area 9010 is assigned to storage type 9010 under Labor management

2: 17UN is assigned to external step process step under labor management

3: Because of above two steps Labor management is active

4: As per determined engineered labor standard, condition CON1(weight < 50), planned delivery is 7 Min.

H2.3.1.3: Planned load(/SCWM/MON)

As soon as WO is created, planned load will be populating and can be seen under labor management.

H2.3.1.4: Assigned WO to Resource/User S4EWM77

Done with /SCWM/MON

H2.3.1.5: Confirm WO: using /SCWM/RFUI or /SCWM/MON

H2.3.1.6: Executed workload:

After WO confirmation, executed workload can be viewed in labor management under /SCWM/MON

1: Actual duration = 20.53

2: Adj Planned = 7

3: efficiency = (7 / 20.53) X 100 = 34.09

H2.3.2: Scenario 2: Weight is greater than 50 KG

H2.3.2.1 : Inbound delivery: 

Created with tcode VL31N with Handling unit(pallet)

H2.3.2.2 Warehouse order 1446(created using /SCWM/PRDI)

H2.3.2.3 :  Planned load(/SCWM/MON)

As soon as WO is created, planned load will be populating and can be seen under labor management.

1: As per determined engineered labor standard, condition CON1(weight > 50), planned delivery is 2 * 7 = 14 Min.

H2.3.2.4 : WO is assigned resource and confirmed Done with /SCWM/MON

H2.3.2.5 : Executed workload: 

After WO confirmation, executed workload can be viewed in labor management under /SCWM/MON

1: Actual duration = 31

2: Adj Planned = 14

3: efficiency = (14 / 31) X 100 = 45.16

Scenario#3 : Indirect Labor:

If warehouse operator is not doing direct task (WO) and having some meeting or maintenance activity, which are not planned, then this scenario will be in scope. This can be created with or without activity area. Below was created without activity area. Under this task, they would be punching start and end time. This also comes to picture when evaluating the performance of a warehouse operator.

H2.3.4: Scenario4: Executed workload

This report can be viewed in labor management under /SCWM/MON

 

H2.3.5: Scenario 5: Overall efficiency(/SCWM/SAPLWIP_EWRKL)

Aggregate efficiency can be populated using this report and can also be viewed by No aggregation, Date and activity area, Date proc and activity, External process steps, Date entitled and steps, Date and reference, Date and processor.

1: Sum of two direct tasks 10170000001445 + 10170000001445

Actual direct task time = 20 + 31 = 51

Planned direct task time = 07 + 14 = 21

Efficiency = (21/51)*100 = 40.75

2: Indirect Task

Planned=Actual task time = 17

Efficiency = (17/17) * 100=100

H3.0 Conclusion:

The implementation of Labor Management in SAP EWM provides a powerful framework for designing, result capturing and finally optimizing labor workforce performance in warehouse operations. By adding detailed steps in configuration, master data, execution, testing, and reporting, it can achieve greater transparency in labor utilization.

Proper use of planning activity areas engineered labor standards(ELS), and resource-based execution, SAP EWM enables accurate measurement of both direct and indirect activities. As demonstrated in this article, a structured and well-configured Labor Management solution can significantly enhance the operational efficiency and decision-making capabilities. For SAP consultants, this will be a very good document to simulate the process, design and implement labor management process in warehouse in an excellent way.

This is very critical process in SAP EWM and can also integrated in Human Resource department (HRD), wherein performance report can be shared with HRD team, if this is not calculated correctly then warehouse wage/salary will be impacted. ELS should well determine the planned task time with proper studies (Time and motion study) and before submitting the performance report with HRD, this needs to be well verified. 

Important considerations:

  • Incase internet is not good then will slow down the process and will delay the actual time and will impact the performance.
  • Planned time is not correct, then also performance will be impacted.
  • As this is very critical process and impact salary for warehouse person, so should be careful designed and well checked before finalizing this process.
  • Material should have correct weight, especially when a new material master is created

I would advise to consultant approaching this for the first time, should do the following:

  • Obtain practical planned data from the business and ensure that it is realistic.
  • Encourage the business to involve warehouse team from day one so that they are fully engaged. This will help build their confidence in the process.
  • Work closely with the business team to define appropriate KPIs, ensuring they gain the trust of warehouse team.
    Clearly communicate to warehouse team that the system is not intended for punishment, but for improving warehouse processes.

Incorrect ELS time estimation caused a real business problem because planned data was simply copied from other sites. And this will create so many problems to warehouse personnel like under performing, Morale decreased and finally lost the confidence in Labor management.

 

Author: Naveen Chandra Sharma

(Naveen is SAP Solution architect having 29+ years of experience which includes 20+ years into SAP consulting with expertise in SAP ECC, SAP S/4HANA and integration with other external system like MES, CIMCORP etc. He worked on various type of project flavor like Implementation, Enhancement and support projects in various industries Manufacturing, Finance, Pharmaceutical, Food, Telecommunication)

entry.title: Setting Your SAP TM and SAP EWM Project Up for Success – Join Our Continuous Quality Check Pilots!
entry.content:
Are you looking to ensure your SAP Transportation Management (SAP TM) and SAP Extended Warehouse Management (SAP EWM) implementation projects are on the right path? Would you like to gain clarity on critical topics, best practices, and potential challenges before they impact your project timeline?

If so, we have excellent news for you!

SAP Enterprise Support, via the Enterprise Support Advisory Council, is excited to announce two new pilot offerings designed to help your organization achieve implementation excellence through our Continuous Quality Check (CQC) for transformation assessment for SAP TM and SAP EWM.

About the Pilots:

The CQC for transformation assessment is conducted during the SAP Activate "Prepare" phase. This service delivers:

  • Evaluation of your project team's readiness by assessing awareness and knowledge of critical SAP TM and SAP EWM topics
  • Gap analysis SAP-recommended approaches
  • Individualized recommendations, including best practices, enablement resources, and actionable guidance
  • Strategic clarity on key implementation topics, common project challenges, integration requirements, and system considerations

Whether you're implementing SAP TM, SAP EWM, or both solutions together, this assessment will provide valuable insights to strengthen your project foundation, covering essential content areas such as:

  • System availability and performance
  • Project preparation
  • Solution-specific best practices

Who Should Participate?

This pilot is ideal for organizations that:

  • Are in the SAP Activate "Prepare" phase for SAP TM and/or SAP EWM
  • Have assembled a project team ready to begin or currently planning their implementation
  • Want to validate their implementation approach against SAP best practices

Ready to Transform Your Implementation?

If your organization meets the criteria above, don't miss this opportunity to join a new pilot service designed to maximize your SAP investment and ensure project success.

Reach out today to the SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council for detailed information and take the first step toward a successful, efficient implementation.

We look forward to partnering with you on this exciting journey to operational excellence.

More information:

  • SAP EWM - short video here.

Get involved? Be part of the innovation!

Enhance Your SAP Experience with Value Maps

SAP Enterprise Support offers additional resources to help you drive innovation. Value maps on SAP Community bring together guidance, recommended actions, and supporting resources to help you decide what to focus on next - all included in your SAP cloud subscription.
