entry.title: SAP EWM Basic vs Advanced: Risk based Assessment Criteria for CPG Warehousing Scenarios
entry.content:
Summary

When organisations assess SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM), the evaluation often starts with a simple question: “Does Basic EWM fulfil our functional requirements?”

While this is a valid starting point, experience from multiple SAP S/4HANA engagements shows that functional coverage alone is not a sufficient decision criterion. In Consumer Packaged Goods (CPG) environments in particular, warehouse operations tend to evolve rapidly due to seasonality, promotion‑driven peaks, SKU proliferation, and service‑level pressure.

This blog provides a risk‑based assessment framework to help architects and programme leaders decide when Advanced EWM should be considered, even if Basic EWM appears sufficient today.

It complements the SAP Community article “SAP EWM Deployment Options – Embedded vs De-central Warehouse Management” by focusing specifically on capability scope within Embedded EWM rather than deployment architecture. Reference: SAP EWM Deployment Options – Embedded vs Decentral Warehouse Management

 

Background

SAP S/4HANA offers two strategic deployment models for Extended Warehouse Management:

  • Embedded EWM, running within the S/4HANA system (Basic or Advanced)
  • Decentralised EWM, running on a separate S/4HANA instance

The choice between Embedded and Decentralised EWM is primarily an enterprise architecture decision, driven by factors such as:

  • Multi‑ERP integration
  • Performance isolation
  • Automation intensity
  • Independent upgrade cycles

These considerations are explained in detail in the SAP Community Enterprise Architecture Knowledge Base article referenced above. However, within Embedded EWM, customers must make an additional decision that is often underestimated:

Should Embedded EWM be implemented with a Basic or an Advanced licence?

Because Basic EWM is included with SAP S/4HANA, many programmes default to it without fully assessing future warehouse risk and evolution, particularly in industries such as CPG where operational volatility is the norm rather than the exception.

 

Assessing Basic vs Advanced EWM: Beyond Functional Fit

Reframing the Decision: Execution vs Optimisation

The distinction between Basic and Advanced EWM is not only about which features are technically available at go‑live.

  • Basic EWM supports stable warehouse execution for predictable processes and limited optimisation needs.
  • Advanced EWM supports continuous optimisation, higher throughput, and evolving business models.

In CPG warehouses, requirements rarely remain static. A site that starts with straightforward pallet handling may later need to support:

  • Promotion‑driven surge volumes
  • Mixed‑case picking
  • Faster outbound cut‑off times
  • Increased customer segmentation

The key assessment question therefore becomes: Is the warehouse expected to remain execution‑focused, or will optimisation and adaptability become critical?

Key Assessment Criteria

Below are common conditions under which Advanced EWM should be evaluated, even when Basic EWM meets current scope.

Business Criticality

In CPG supply chains, regional and central distribution centres often sit directly on the critical path to revenue.
Missed despatch windows during promotions or peak seasons can have immediate commercial impact.

Advanced EWM reduces operational risk in warehouses where service levels directly affect retail availability.

Demand Volatility and Seasonality

CPG warehouses typically face:

  • Strong seasonality (e.g. summer beverages, festive demand)
  • Sudden demand spikes driven by promotions
  • Rapid SKU introductions and retirements

Advanced EWM offers greater flexibility to manage volume‑driven variability without redesigning core processes later.

Shift from FIFO Execution to Throughput Optimisation

Many CPG sites start with basic FIFO picking but later require:

  • Prioritised outbound waves
  • Dynamic work assignment for peak periods
  • Improved visibility into warehouse workload

These optimisation patterns are inherently better supported withAdvanced EWM.

Automation as a Medium‑Term Option

Even if automation is not part of the initial scope, CPG organisations frequently evaluate:

  • Conveyor integration
  • Automated pallet handling
  • High‑speed picking areas for fast movers

As highlighted in SAP architecture guidance, highly automated or high‑throughput warehouses typically require advanced orchestration and control capabilities, particularly as complexity increases.

Licensing and Compliance Considerations

Certain EWM design patterns can implicitly activate advanced functionality, creating potential licence compliance risk if only Basic EWM is contractually covered.

From a governance perspective, selecting Advanced EWM upfront removes ambiguity, particularly in large multi‑site CPG programmes.

 

Risk‑Based Decision Model

Rather than relying solely on feature checklists, we may want to apply a risk‑based assessment per warehouse.

Typical assessment questions: Score each site YES / NO

  • Will volumes / SKUs / Channels / picking-complexity increase?
  • Is peak performance business‑critical?
  • Are promotions or seasonal surges difficult to absorb today?
  • Is future automation plausible?
  • Is audit / licence risk unacceptable?
  • Would architectural flexibility be required later? e.g. Might need to be decentralised later?

Guideline outcome:

  • Low risk (0–1 YES) → Basic EWM may be sufficient
  • Medium risk (2–3 YES) → Advanced EWM strongly recommended
  • High risk (4+ YES) → Advanced EWM effectively mandatory

This approach aligns well with CPG environments where change is expected rather than exceptional.

 

Conclusion

In CPG supply chains, warehouse requirements rarely remain unchanged after go‑live. Promotions, seasonality, customer demands, and network optimisation continuously increase operational pressure.

While Basic EWM can be a valid tactical choice for stable, low‑risk warehouses, Advanced EWM provides strategic resilience where growth, volatility, or optimisation are expected.

When used together with the architectural guidance in the SAP Community article on Embedded vs Decentral EWM deployment, organisations can make a coherent, forward‑looking decision that avoids costly redesigns later.

Ultimately, the most successful EWM programmes are those that treat capability choice as a business risk decision, not just a functional one.

Typical Decision Guidance

  • Choose EWM Basic if:
    • Manual operations dominate
    • SKU velocity is low
    • Process orchestration requirements are limited
    • Cost sensitivity is high
  • Choose EWM Advanced if:
    • There are high throughput and/or significant peak volumes
    • Automation or robotics is planned or already in place
    • Labour productivity optimisation is a key objective
    • Complex outbound orchestration is required (e.g. waves, cross-docking, value-added services)
    • There is an opportunity to reduce the existing WM custom code base rather than migrate it
      • For example, if bespoke developments (such as wave management solutions) exist across a subset of warehouses, adopting EWM Advanced for those locations can enable a return to standard functionality
      • This avoids carrying forward custom developments into S/4, thereby supporting a clean core approach and reducing both migration effort and long-term maintenance overhead
    • There is an intention to leverage advanced and emerging capabilities, including any current or future AI-driven capabilities and AI Agents

entry.title: A Deep Dive into Planned Replenishment in SAP EWM
entry.content:
1. Introduction

Even a minor delay in retrieval can lead to a domino effect of downtime and significant financial hits.

To keep things moving, SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) offers robust replenishment strategies that ensure picking bins never run dry.

I’m writing this blog to show how Planned Replenishment effectively bridges the gap between high-rack reserve storage and primary picking zones. The goal is to create a seamless flow of goods so that frequently requested items are always within reach the moment an order is placed.

2. Understanding Replenishment: The Five Strategic Pillars
Replenishment in SAP EWM is the strategic movement of stock from a bulk or reserve area to a designated fixed bin to facilitate efficient picking. While the objective remains the same, the trigger for these movements varies. The following table summarizes the five primary replenishment methods:

Replenishment Type Description

  • Planned Replenishment Triggered by minimum quantity thresholds at fixed bins; executed at scheduled intervals or via batch jobs.
  • Order-Related Replenishment Driven by specific outbound order requirements; tasks are created immediately after picking tasks.
  • Crate Part Replenishment Focused on Production Supply Area (PSA) integration; typically used in Kanban and PP workflows.
  • Automatic Replenishment System-driven background workflow triggered automatically once stock falls below the minimum threshold.
  • Direct Replenishment Operator-driven process initiated via exception codes during the picking process; no additional approval required.

3. The Logic of Planned Replenishment
Planned Replenishment is a proactive strategy widely adopted across various industry verticals. It functions by monitoring "Minimum Quantity" thresholds maintained at the fixed bin level. When the system detects that stock has dropped below this limit, it identifies a deficit.

From a consultant's perspective, the value of this method lies in its predictability. By utilizing batch jobs, the system can generate replenishment warehouse tasks during low-activity periods, ensuring the picking area is fully staged for the upcoming shift without manual intervention or operational bottlenecks.

4. Step-by-Step Configuration
To implement a reliable Planned Replenishment process in Warehouse TECO, the following back-end configurations must be maintained.
1. Activate Replenishment Strategy in Storage Type
Path: EWM → Internal Warehouse Movements → Replenishment Control → Activate Replenishment Strategy in Storage Type
The system must know which storage types are eligible for restocking. For Warehouse TECO, we activate multiple replenishment strategies across different storage types. The overview below shows all configured entries:

For Storage Type 0050 (Fixed Bin Storage), we activate the following four strategies, each serving a different replenishment scenario. For each strategy, Warehouse Process Type 3010 is assigned and "Qty Type Used" is set to Physical Quantity, and "WT Immed." is checked:

Planned Replenishment - Storage Type 0050 (Fixed Bin Storage):

Automatic Replenishment - Storage Type 0050 (Fixed Bin Storage):

Order-Related Replenishment - Storage Type 0050 (Fixed Bin Storage):

Crate Part Replenishment - Storage Type 1000 (Production Supply):

2. Maintain Document and Item Types for Replenishment Warehouse Request
Path: EWM → Internal Warehouse Movements → Replenishment Control → Maintain Document Type / Item Type for Replenishment Warehouse Request
This configuration defines the Warehouse Request that the system generates to move the stock. For Warehouse TECO, Document Type SRPL and Item Type SRPL are assigned for WPT 3010 (Replenishment), and Document Type SPS / Item Type SDPS are assigned for WPT 3100 (Crate Part Replenishment). This ensures the system treats the movement as an internal replenishment rather than a standard outbound delivery.

3. Define Warehouse Process Type (WPT 3010 – Replenishment)
Path: EWM → Cross-Process Settings → Warehouse Task → Define Warehouse Process Type
Define WPT 3010 "Replenishment." Within the WPT settings, the Warehouse Process Category is set to "3" (Internal Warehouse Movement) and the Activity is "REPL." Crucially, the Doc. Type Whse-Intrnl and Item Type Whse-Intrnl must be linked to SRPL to bridge the warehouse request to the physical movement.

4. Define Control Indicators for Determining WPT
Path: EWM → Cross-Process Settings → Warehouse Task → Define Control Indicators for Determining WPT
Control indicators link the product type determination to the warehouse process type. For Warehouse TECO, indicator FX is configured for "Inbound with Unload and put away SMP ZJJA." This indicator is referenced in the product master and is critical for ensuring the system selects the correct WPT during replenishment movements.

5. Determination of Warehouse Process Type
Path: EWM → Cross-Process Settings → Warehouse Task → Determination of Warehouse Process Type
This configuration maps Document Type and Control Indicator combinations to the correct WPT. For Warehouse TECO, Document Type SRPL with Process Type Determination Indicator FX maps to WPT 3010 with "No Special Process" indicator. This drives the system to correctly determine WPT 3010 whenever a replenishment warehouse request is processed.

6. Define Fixed Storage Bin with Min/Max Quantities
T Code: /SCWM/BINMAT
A Fixed Bin must be assigned for the product in the relevant storage type, along with Maximum Quantity and Minimum Quantity thresholds. For Product CABLES in Storage Type 0050, we define Maximum Quantity = 100 EA and Minimum Quantity = 10 EA. The Planned Replenishment run will trigger whenever the physical quantity drops below 10 EA, and will replenish up to the maximum of 100 EA.

7. Maintain Warehouse Product Master Data (PTDI, PACI, SRCI)
T.Code: /SCWM/MAT1
The product master holds the critical indicators that drive both stock put away and stock removal logic during replenishment:
● Process Type Determination Indicator (PTDI): Set to FX -ensures the correct WPT is selected during movement.
● Put away Control Indicator (PACI): Set to YFIX - this is the link to the Storage Type Search Sequence for Pettaway, directing stock to Storage Type 0050 as the replenishment destination.
● Stock Removal Control Indicator (SRCI): Set to SRCI - defines the picking/removal logic from the reserve area (Storage Type 0010).

The PACI indicator YFIX is the critical link. When replenishment is triggered, the system sees YFIX and references the Storage Type Search Sequence to determine that Storage Type 0050 is the correct destination for the stock. Similarly, SRCI drives the removal of stock from the reserve area (Storage Type 0010).

8. Define Putaway Control Indicator (PACI)
Path: EWM → Strategies → Put away → Define Putaway Control Indicator

The Putaway Control Indicator YFIX is configured for Warehouse TECO and mapped to Storage Type 0050 (Fixed Bin Storage). This indicator instructs the system to direct incoming replenishment stock to the correct storage type during putaway determination.

9. Define Storage Type Search Sequence for Put Away
Path: EWM → Strategies → Put away → Define Storage Type Search Sequence for Put away
The Storage Type Search Sequence for Put Away defines the order in which the system searches for destination storage types when performing a put away. For TECO/0050 (YFIX indicator), the search sequence points to Storage Type 0050 as Sequence No. 1, ensuring replenishment stock is directed to the Fixed Bin storage type.

10. Define Stock Removal Control Indicator (SRCI)

Path: EWM → Strategies → Stock Removal → Define Stock Removal Control Indicator
The Stock Removal Control Indicator SRCI is configured as "STOCK REMOVAL FOR REPL" for Warehouse TECO. This indicator is assigned in the product master and is referenced during the stock removal search to determine which storage type to pull stock from during replenishment.

11. Determine Storage Type Search Sequence for Stock Removal
Path: EWM → Strategies → Stock Removal → Determine Storage Type Search Sequence: Stock Removal
For Warehouse TECO, Stock Removal Control Indicator SRCI is mapped to WPT 3010 with Stock Removal Strategy "REPL." This configuration instructs the system to pull stock from Storage Type 0010 (the high-rack reserve area) when executing a replenishment movement to the Fixed Bin.

The detailed assignment confirms that the REPL storage type search sequence assigns Storage Type 0010 as Sequence No. 1 - the source of stock for the replenishment moves:

5. Execution Walkthrough: Testing the Replenishment Run
To validate the configuration, we perform an end-to-end test for Product CABLES in Warehouse TECO.
Pre-Requisite: Initial Stock Verification
Before executing the replenishment run, we verify the current stock position in the Warehouse Management Monitor. As shown, Storage Type 0010 (reserve/high-rack area) holds 100 EA of CABLES, while Storage Type 0050 (FIXED BIN) holds only 10 EA  exactly at the minimum threshold. Since Planned Replenishment triggers when stock is below the minimum (not equal to it), we must first confirm that stock has dropped below 10 EA.

Execute the Replenishment Run
T.Code: /N/SCWM/REPL

Navigate to T Code /SCWM/REPL. Enter the following selection criteria:
● Warehouse Number: TECO
● Replenishment Strategy: Planned Replenishment
● Party Entitled to Dispose: 1000487
● Product: CABLES

First Run - No Replenishment Required
In the first execution, the system log confirms the replenishment run was started, but returns the message "No replenishment to execute." This is expected behavior: the FIXED BIN currently holds 10 EA, which equals the configured minimum quantity. Since the stock has not yet dropped below the threshold, no deficit is detected, and no warehouse task is created.

Second Run - Deficit Detected, WT Created
After stock in the FIXED BIN is consumed below the 10 EA minimum threshold (e.g., through a picking operation reducing it to 5 EA), the replenishment run is executed again. This time, the system identifies the deficit and proposes the replenishment items:

The system proposes a replenishment quantity of 95 EA for CABLES in Storage Type 0050 (FIXED BIN), using WPT 3010 with immediate WT creation. This quantity bridges the gap from the current 5 EA to the configured maximum of 100 EA (5 + 95 = 100).
The log confirms successful warehouse task creation:

Warehouse Task 603 - Movement Details
Reviewing the generated Warehouse Task 603 in the monitor, we can see the full movement details. The system has created a task to move stock from Source Bin 011.01.01.04 (Storage Type 0010 - high-rack reserve) to Destination Bin FIXED BIN (Storage Type 0050), using Process Type 3010:

The task quantity detail confirms a Source Target Quantity of 95 EA:

Confirm Warehouse Task
To physically execute the stock movement, select WT 603 and choose "Confirm WT in Background" from the context menu. This confirms the warehouse task and completes the stock transfer from the reserve area to the Fixed Bin:

6. Results and Final Verification
After confirming the warehouse task, a final check of the Warehouse Management Monitor confirms that the replenishment has been executed successfully. The "Unrestricted-Use Warehouse" stock in Storage Type 0050 (FIXED BIN) is now exactly 100 EA - fully restocked to the configured maximum. The reserve area (Storage Type 0010) correspondingly reflects 10 EA remaining:

Warehouse Task 603 was created automatically by the Planned Replenishment run. • The system calculated a replenishment quantity of 95 EA, the exact amount needed to reach the Maximum Quantity of 100 EA from the deficit level of 5 EA. • The movement was executed from Source Bin 011.01.01.04 (Storage Type 0010) to Destination Bin FIXED BIN (Storage Type 0050). • Final stock in FIXED BIN: 100 EA fully replenished

7. Conclusion
Through this walkthrough, we validated how SAP EWM Planned Replenishment leverages a chain of tightly integrated configurations - storage type activation, fixed bin min/max settings, process type determination, put away, and stock removal search sequences - to create a fully automated and reliable stock replenishment workflow.

The Planned Replenishment strategy is particularly well-suited for warehouses with predictable demand patterns, such as automotive spare parts environments, where ensuring fixed bin availability is critical to preventing production downtime. When combined with scheduled batch jobs, it provides a hands-free, proactive mechanism that keeps picking locations stocked without manual intervention.

entry.title: EWM Inbound process with POSC - Deconsolidation
entry.content:
EWM Inbound process with POSC - Deconsolidation

Introduction:

This is the most common scenario where, vendor sends a single pallet containing five different SKUs. And we can't put the whole pallet in one bin because SKU A goes to Cold Storage, SKU B goes to General Shelving, and SKU C is Hazardous for example. This POSC solution directs the pallet to a Deconsolidation Work Center. There, a worker scans the items and "re-packs" them into smaller, destination-specific HUs and moved to respective storage bins.

And this deconsolidation steps are applicable for certain products from specific vendors where vendors usually sends the different SKU's within single pallet. This will be control by process type determination indicator.  For standard products direct putawy can be carried out.


Configurations Steps:

  1. Define Warehouse Process type
  2. Determine Process type determination indicator
  3. Determine Warehouse process type
  4. Putaway Strategies

4.1 Define Storage Type Search Sequence for Putaway

4.2 Assign Storage Types to Storage Type Search Sequence

4.3 Define Putaway Control Indicator

4.4 Specify Storage Type Search Sequence for Putaway

4.5 Optimize Access Strategy for Storage Type Search: Putaway

  1. Define Process Oriented Storage Control

5.1 Define external steps

5.2 Storage process - Definition

5.3 Assign Storage step process to Storage process

5.4 Process Oriented Storage Control - Setup

  1. Specify Work center Layout
  2. Define Work Center
  3. Assign bin to Work Center
  4. Assign Storage bin to Activity areas
  5. Deconsolidation in Goods receipt process

10.1 Define Attributes of deconsolidation

10.2 Specify deconsolidation station

10.3 Assign number range intervals to consolidation groups

 

Configuration Path:

  1. Define Warehouse Process type

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM->Cross-process settings-> Warehouse task-> Define warehouse process type

Standard Warehouse Porcess type for putawy-PW02

The movement the Goods are arrived from vendor it will be approached in the door and from the unloading will be done to move the pallet for deconsolidation.

Internal warehouse process types used in POSC 3060 and 3065

  1. Determine Process type determination indicator

 Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM->Cross-process settings-> Warehouse task-> Define Control Indicators for Determining Warehouse Process Types

  1. Determine Warehouse process type

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM -> Cross-process settings-> Warehouse task-> Determine Warehouse Process Type

  1. Putaway Strategies

4.1 Define Storage Type Search Sequence for Putaway

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM -> Goods Receipt Process ->Strategies -> Storage Type Search-> Define Storage Type Search Sequence for Putaway

4.2 Assign Storage Types to Storage Type Search Sequence

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM -> Goods Receipt Process ->Strategies -> Storage Type Search-> Assign Storage Types to Storage Type Search Sequence

4.3 Define Putaway Control Indicator

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM -> Goods Receipt Process ->Strategies -> Storage Type Search-> Define Putaway Control Indicator

4.4 Specify Storage Type Search Sequence for Putaway

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM -> Goods Receipt Process-> Strategies -> Storage Type Search-> Specify Storage Type Search Sequence for Putaway

4.5 Optimize Access Strategy for Storage Type Search: Putaway

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM -> Goods Receipt Process ->Strategies -> Storage Type Search-> Optimize Access Strategy for Storage Type Search: Putaway

  1. Define Process Oriented Storage Control

5.1 Define external steps

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM->Cross-process settings-> Warehouse task-> Define Process-Oriented Storage Control

5.2 Storage process - Definition

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM->Cross-process settings-> Warehouse task-> Define Process-Oriented Storage Control

5.3 Assign Storage step process to Storage process

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM->Cross-process settings-> Warehouse task-> Define Process-Oriented Storage Control

Make sure to assign this Storage process to the Warehouse process type PW02

5.4 Process Oriented Storage Control - setup

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM->Cross-process settings-> Warehouse task-> Define Process-Oriented Storage Control

  1. Specify Work center Layout

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM ->Master data -> Work Center -> Specify Work Center Layout

  1. Define Work Center

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM ->Master data -> Work Center -> Define Work Center

  1. Assign bin to Work Center

Tcode: /N/SCWM/TWORKST

  1. Assign Storage bin to Activity areas

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM ->Master data -> Activity Areas -> Assign Storage Bins to Activity Areas.

  1. Deconsolidation in Goods receipt process

10.1 Define Attributes of deconsolidation

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM -> Goods Receipt Process -> Deconsolidation-> Define Attributes for Deconsolidation

10.2 Specify Deconsolidation Station

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM -> Goods Receipt Process -> Deconsolidation-> Specify Deconsolidation Station

10.3 Assign number range intervals to Consolidation Groups

Spro-> Ref IMG-> SCM EWM->EWM -> Goods Receipt Process -> Deconsolidation-> Assign Number Range Intervals to Consolidation Groups

Testing:

Test Materials are 17H1-EWM-03, 17H1-EWM-04, 17H1-EWM-05

Make sure we have assigned Process type determination indicator PS has been assigned

Inbound delivery has been created and distributed to EWM

IBD: 180106588

Access Inbound delivery in EWM /n/SCWM/PRDI

All 3 Products are packed in single pallet.

Create warehouse task for unloading from Door

Now lets access this Inbound delivery in Monitor /n/SCWM/MON

As you can see 4 different tasks are created, where one task 221 is open and other tasks are in status B - Waiting

Lets confirm the open task,

The moment the task is confirmed, new task has been created for Deconsolidation

Lets confirm the Deconsolidation task, and perform the deconsolidation activity

Access Transaction code /n/SCWM/DCONS

input details as below and execute

We can a pallet with 3 Products

Now create 3 individual HU to pack individually.

991000000037; 991000000038; 991000000039

Select task and click ok and click on execute button to pack into destination HU. Repeat the steps for other 2 Products

3 Products are packed to individual HU in Deconsolidation work center.

Now, Go back to monitor and refresh the delivery.

Now final task from Deconsolidation bin to final bin is open to confirm

After confirmation of the tasks, putaway will be completed and stock is updated in the designated bins successfully.

As we mentioned earlier, this deconsolidation process has been controlled for specific products from specific vendor with process type determination indicator which is maintained in the Product Master.

For receiving of standard products are straight forward process,  example for product 17H1-EWM-01, there is no Process type determination indicator maintained.

Putaway completed and stock is updated in designated Storage bin.

 

Conclusion:

This client requirement is effectively addressed through the implementation of SAP EWM Process-Oriented Storage Control (POSC) with Deconsolidation, which provides the necessary flexibility and control for handling complex inbound shipments. By leveraging POSC, the warehouse can systematically manage mixed‑SKU vendor pallets that cannot be safely or accurately stored through standard direct putaway.

The deconsolidation step converts bulk inbound Handling Units into destination‑specific HUs, ensuring that materials with special storage requirements—such as cold chain items, hazardous materials, or regulated products—are automatically routed to their respective storage types or work centers. This structured transition significantly improves bin-level inventory accuracy and eliminates the risk of incorrect or unsafe storage.

The use of the Process Type Determination Indicator plays a key role in differentiating complex mixed pallets from simple single‑SKU loads. As a result, POSC activities are triggered only when required, ensuring that standard inbound flows continue uninterrupted while operational resources are efficiently allocated to deconsolidation scenarios. The POSC Setup, minimizes manual decision‑making on the warehouse floor, reduces handling errors, and ensures consistent execution of putaway rules.

 

***End of the Document***

entry.title: HU Close step in pack work center in RF with Outbound POSC in SAP EWM- Scenario
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION :

Process oriented Storage control is a feature which we extensively use in SAP EWM .It brings processes in consideration while building the process flow. In the configuration we have 'Auto  WT'  flag and we know that if we flag it ,then it creates the WT of the next process step automatically in the background queue method. But in reality what happens behind the scenes, we will try to see in this blog.

This blog will  give the basic understanding of the 'Auto WT' creation flag in POSC setting with HU  close option in RF (also in GUI in  general) and what happens if we miss to enable that. Will take step by step with scenarios what happens if this check is not flagged and when flagged.

The storage process ZPCK has 3 steps - Pick(OB01), Pack (ZOB2) and stage (ZOB3)

The Pack work center bin gets determined from the /SCWM/PACKSTDT - Determine Work Center in Goods Issue 

The storage process is assigned to WOCR and the  search sequence is with AA- CB01,

 

The example  delivery have 2 EA of pick quantity, where the source storage type is 0050, Pack work station is 8030 and the GI staging area is 9020.

Note :

The picking (OB01) step in this example is completed with a pick hu, which is confirmed to pack work center.

Will start the  scenarios with pack step (ZOB2)

Scenario 1: No Auto WT in ZOB2 step

Close HU step via RF, triggers the FM -/SCWM/RF_PACK_HU_CLOSE_PAI

The variable GT_MOVEHU is not updated if the ls_process_assignment-autfo field is not set, 

which is the Auto WT flag in the POSC step configuration.

This then goes in the BAdI /SCWM/EX_WRKC_UI_AFTER_SAVE  where it checks this field and if IT_MOVEHU is empty then it skips the entire logic to create the WT. Therefore, there won't be any automatic WT for  ZOB3  step.

Note in this case- user needs to manually trigger the WT via ad-hoc  moves

Scenario 2 : Auto WT checked in Z0B2 step


The ls_process_assignment-autfo  is not initial now. It then updates the GT_MOVEHU field.

The control then goes to the badi /SCWM/EX_WRKC_UI_AFTER_SAVE where it does check the POSC step and then triggers the WT in background queue with queue name with WMTH*

WT gets created automatically in background.

Important SAP NOTES:

3091718 - Close HU does not create the follow-up WT automatically

(https://me.sap.com/notes/3091718/E)

 

CONCLUSION :

This completes the blog. This blogs helps to understand the backend logic of HU Close and  'Auto WT' feature in POSC scenario. Sometimes missing a small check box makes it challenging to figure out. Hope it helps.

 

Thanks for your time. Please like and share your valuable comments.

Best Regards,

Y K B B Rudra Narayan

entry.title: Ensuring Inventory Accuracy End-to-End Configuration & Execution Guide (Physical Inventory)
entry.content:
Physical Inventory in SAP EWM is the structured process of comparing physical (actual) stock counts against book inventory (system-recorded quantities) to identify and correct discrepancies. This document covers the full configuration and execution of Physical Inventory in Warehouse PINT, demonstrated using product LAPTOPS (PINT COMPANY LAPTOP) as a real-world test scenario.

1. What Is Physical Inventory?

Physical Inventory is performed to verify that the system stock quantities match what is physically present in the warehouse. Discrepancies can arise due to picking errors, damage, theft, system posting errors, or unreported stock movements.

SAP EWM supports two counting approaches: Storage-Bin-Specific counting (count everything in a bin) and Product-Specific counting (count a specific product across bins). Both approaches follow the same document-driven workflow.

2. Physical Inventory Procedure Types

SAP EWM supports five Physical Inventory procedure types, each suited for different operational scenarios:

Annual Physical Inventory---AL---Full warehouse count executed once a year.
Cycle Counting---CC---Rolling count of subsets, reducing disruption.
Ad-hoc Physical Inventory---HL---On-demand count for specific bins or products.
Low Stock Check---LS---Triggered when stock falls below a threshold.
Put away PI---PI---Auto triggered during put away operations.

Focus of this document: Ad-hoc Physical Inventory (Storage-Bin-Specific) - the most commonly used approach for targeted, on-demand counts of specific bins when discrepancies are suspected, without requiring a full warehouse shutdown.

3. Physical Inventory Process Flow

The complete Physical Inventory process follows five sequential steps from document creation through final stock adjustment in S/4HANA:

Create PI Document ---- /SCWM/PI_CREATE ---Generate a Physical Inventory document for selected bins or products.
Count the Stock ---/SCWM/PI_PROCESS---- Enter the physically counted quantities against book inventory.
Post the Document ----/SCWM/PI_PROCESS--- Post the count — system calculates differences vs. book inventory.
Analyze Differences ---/SCWM/DIFF_ANALYZER ---Review and approve differences in the Difference Analyzer.
Clear & Update S/4HANA ---/SCWM/DIFF_ANALYZER ----Differences cleared and automatically posted back to S/4HANA.

4. Configuration
The following configurations must be completed in SAP EWM before Physical Inventory can be executed in Warehouse PINT.

1. Define Physical Inventory Area
Path: EWM → Physical Inventory → Physical Inventory Area → Define Physical Inventory Area
Physical Inventory Areas (PINT_0010 through PINT_0040) are defined to organize and group storage bins for counting. Each area is configured with behaviors such as Posting After Count, HU Complete, and HU Count. The overview shows all configured areas for Warehouse PINT:

 

 

 

Physical Inventory Areas overview - PINT_0010 to PINT_0040

The detailed overview shows the full list of Physical Inventory Areas configured across the system, including their complete settings for Put away PI, LSPI, LS Check, Tolerance Check, and HU Count:

Full Physical Inventory Area list showing all configured entries

For each PI Area, permitted document types (procedures) are assigned. PINT_0010 allows Annual, Cycle Counting, Ad-hoc, and Low Stock procedures:

Permitted Document Types for PINT_0010

2. Assign Physical Inventory Area to Activity Area
Path: EWM → Physical Inventory → Physical Inventory Area → Assign Physical Inventory Area to Activity Area
Each PI Area is assigned to its corresponding Activity Area to link the counting zones to the warehouse bin structure. Activity Areas 0010–0040 map to PI Areas PINT_0010–PINT_0040, respectively:

Assignment of PI Areas to Activity Areas

3. Define Number Ranges for Physical Inventory Document
Path: EWM → Physical Inventory → Define Number Range for Physical Inventory Document
Number Range 01 is configured for the year 2026 on Object /SCWM/PIDO, Subobject PINT, covering the full range of PI document numbers required for the warehouse year:

Number Range for PI Documents - Year 2026, Range 01

4. Specify Physical Inventory-Specific Settings in Warehouse
Path: EWM → Physical Inventory → Specify Physical Inventory-Specific Settings in Warehouse
Warehouse-level PI settings define currency (USD), fiscal year variant (K4), procedure ID (0001), and key behavioral flags. For Warehouse PINT, Owner and Party to Dispose are enabled, Corr. Perm. is checked (allowing corrections), and the NRI PI Document is set to 01:

Physical Inventory-Specific Settings for Warehouse PINT

5. Define Tolerance Groups
Three types of tolerance groups control how differences are handled at different stages and by different user roles. All three must be configured:

a) Tolerance Group for Recounting
Path: EWM → Physical Inventory → Define Tolerance Group for Recounting
Defines when a recount is required based on percentage or value. CLERK: 20% / USD 1,000. SUPERVISOR: 50% / USD 1,000. MANAGER: 100% / USD 10,000 (no recount required):

Tolerance Groups for Recounting - CLERK, MANAGER, SUPERVISOR

b) Tolerance Group for Posting Differences
Path: EWM → Physical Inventory → Define Tolerance Group for Posting Differences
Controls which differences can be directly posted without additional approval. CLERK: 10% / USD 100. SUPERVISOR: 50% / USD 1,000. MANAGER: 100% / USD 10,000 (unlimited):

Tolerance Groups for Posting Differences - CLERK, MANAGER, SUPERVISOR

c) Tolerance Group for Difference Analyzer
Path: EWM → Physical Inventory → Define Tolerance Group for Difference Analyzer
Defines value thresholds requiring review before stock adjustments post to S/4HANA. CLERK: USD 100. SUPERVISOR: USD 1,000. MANAGER: USD 1,000,000:

Tolerance Groups for Difference Analyzer - CLERK, MANAGER, SUPERVISOR

The three-tier tolerance framework (CLERK → SUPERVISOR → MANAGER) provides a robust approval chain. Small discrepancies are handled by clerks, while larger adjustments require supervisory or management approval — ensuring financial controls are maintained at every level.

6. Define Priorities for Physical Inventory

Path: EWM → Physical Inventory → Define Priorities for Physical Inventory
Three priority levels are configured for Warehouse PINT: 1 = HIGH, 2 = MEDIUM, 3 = LOW. These help supervisors manage the counting workload and urgency:

Physical Inventory Priorities - HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW

7. Define Reason for Physical Inventory
Path: EWM → Physical Inventory → Define Reason for Physical Inventory
Six reasons explain why a physical inventory was initiated. Each is linked to a priority and marked Active: CCIV (Cycle Counting, Priority 2), LSPI (Low Stock Check, Priority 1), PTPI (Putaway PI, Priority 2), STND (Standard, Priority 2), UNAS (Not Assigned, Priority 3), UPLD (Unplanned, Priority 1):

Reasons for Physical Inventory - all six reasons for Warehouse PINT

8. Define Reason for Differences
Path: EWM → Physical Inventory → Define Reason for Differences
When a discrepancy is found, a reason must be assigned to explain it. Five reasons are configured: CUCL (Customer Claim), CORR (Correction), QIDF (Inspection Document), STOC (Stock Transfer), WTDF (Warehouse Task):

Reasons for Differences - five reason codes for Warehouse PINT


5. End-to-End Test: Physical Inventory for LAPTOPS

To validate the configuration, a complete Physical Inventory test is performed for product LAPTOPS (PINT COMPANY LAPTOP) in Storage Bin 0010-01-01, Storage Type 0010, Warehouse PINT. The test demonstrates the full count-to-posting workflow with a deliberate discrepancy.

Pre-Requisite: Verify Initial Stock
Before initiating the Physical Inventory, the Warehouse Management Monitor EWM and in S4  is checked to confirm the book inventory. Storage Bin 0010-01-01 shows 80 EA of LAPTOPS in Unrestricted-Use Warehouse stock (Stock Type F2). This is the book value the count will be compared against:

Warehouse Monitor - Storage Bin 0010-01-01 with 80 EA of LAPTOPS (book inventory)

Step 1: Create Physical Inventory Document

T.Code: /SCWM/PI_CREATE
Navigate to /SCWM/PI_CREATE and create a PI document for Warehouse PINT. Procedure: AL — Annual Physical Inventory (Storage-Bin-Specific). Storage Bin: 0010-01-01. Reason: UPLD (Standard Physical Inventory). Priority: 2 (MEDIUM):


PI Create - Procedure AL, Storage Bin 0010-01-01, Reason UPLD, Priority MEDIUM

The system confirms: "Document 10000 2026 created". PI Document 10000 is created with Status Active:

PI Document 10000 created - Status Active, Procedure HL, Ad-hoc Physical Inventory

Step 2: Process and Enter Count

T.Code: /SCWM/PI_PROCESS
Open PI Document 10000. Enter the physically counted quantity: 78 EA of LAPTOPS were found in the bin (book inventory says 80 EA). Count is entered by user MANAGER:

PI Process - Count Items tab showing 78 EA entered for LAPTOPS in bin 0010-01-01


After saving, the system confirms: "Document 10000 000001 counted." PI Status updates to Counted ;

PI Document after count - Status "Counted", 78 EA recorded

Step 3: Post the Physical Inventory Document
T.Code: /SCWM/PI_PROCESS
The document now shows Action = "Post Difference" with PI Status = Counted. The system has identified a discrepancy: 80 EA book vs. 78 EA counted = -2 EA difference. Click Post to commit the adjustment:

PI Document ready to post Action "Post Difference", Status "Counted."

The system confirms: "Document 10000 000001 posted." PI Status changes to Posted:

PI Document after posting - Status "Posted"

Final posted state - PI Document 10000 fully processed

Step 4: Review and Post via Difference Analyzer
T.Code: /SCWM/DIFF_ANALYZER
Once the PI document is posted, the difference is sent to the Difference Analyzer. Navigate to /SCWM/DIFF_ANALYZER for Warehouse PINT. Open the search dialog and check "PI Difference" to filter for physical inventory differences. The analyzer shows the -2 EA discrepancy for product LAPTOPS:

Difference Analyzer search dialog - PI Difference selected for Warehouse PIN
The detail view confirms the difference: Product LAPTOPS, Difference Qty = 2 EA, Diff. Value = USD 20.00, Priority 1 (HIGH), Posting Date 02/18/2026, Stock Type F2 Unrestricted-Use Warehouse:

Difference Analyzer detail LAPTOPS, 2 EA difference, USD 20.00, Priority HIGH

Select the difference record and click "Post" to apply the inventory adjustment. The system generates a Warehouse Task and confirms: "Warehouse task 23 created" - the stock correction movement is now physically executed:

After confirming the warehouse task.

Difference Analyzer Warehouse Task 23 was created after posting the -2 EA difference

6. Results and Final Verification
After the Difference Analyzer posting, the Warehouse Management Monitor confirms the stock in Storage Bin 0010-01-01 is now 78 EA of LAPTOPS (reduced from 80 EA), with the physical count weight reflected as 78 KG:

Warehouse Monitor Storage Bin 0010-01-01 now at 78 EA / 78 KG after PI adjustment.

The S/4HANA Stock Overview (T.Code: MMBE) confirms the inventory adjustment has been propagated from EWM back to S/4HANA. Total unrestricted stock for LAPTOPS shows 230 EA across the PINT COMPANY / PINT TECHNOLOGY plant. The AFS AVAIL FOR SALE storage location within PINT TECHNOLOGY specifically shows 78 EA, directly reflecting the Physical Inventory count result posted from EWM, confirming full end-to-end synchronisation:

S/4HANA MMBE (T.Code: MMBE) - LAPTOPS total 230 EA unrestricted across PINT COMPANY / PINT TECHNOLOGY plant; AFS AVAIL FOR SALE storage location reflects 78 EA, confirming EWM-to-S/4HANA inventory sync after Physical Inventory adjustment

Key Results Summary: PI Document 10000 created for Bin 0010-01-01 (LAPTOPS) in Warehouse PINT. Book: 80 EA | Count: 78 EA | Difference: -2 EA | Value: USD 20.00. Process: Created → Active → Counted → Posted → Difference Analyzer → Posted (WT 23 created). EWM stock adjusted to 78 EA. S/4HANA MMBE confirmed a total of 230 EA  full EWM-to-S4 integration validated.


7. Conclusion
This walkthrough demonstrated the complete Physical Inventory process in SAP EWM — from system configuration through to stock reconciliation in S/4HANA. The structured approach of Physical Inventory Areas, tolerance groups, priorities, and reason codes ensures that every count discrepancy is properly captured, reviewed, and resolved at the appropriate authorization level.

The three-tier tolerance framework (CLERK → SUPERVISOR → MANAGER) provides essential financial controls - ensuring that significant inventory adjustments always receive appropriate management approval before impacting the books.

For high-volume warehouses, configure Cycle Counting as the primary PI strategy rather than Annual Physical Inventory. By assigning cycle count classes (A/B/C) based on product value and movement frequency, you can continuously maintain inventory accuracy without the disruption of a full annual count, keeping the warehouse operational and the books accurate year-round.

entry.title: 3 Journeys That Redefine Your Logistics in a Disruptive World
entry.content:
The world of logistics is undergoing a profound transformation, driven by volatility, global fragmentation, ongoing supply chain disruptions, and rapid technological change. Workforce scarcity impacting global logistics operations, logistics corridors becoming tools in geopolitical conflicts, and continuously rising transportation costs all point to the same reality: Future-proofing your logistics is no longer a strategic choice, but the critical tipping point that separates those who will rise from those who will fall in an increasingly unforgiving global economy.   

Modernizing logistics is no longer about “if” but about “how fast” and “how far”. 

Traditional logistics systems were designed primarily for efficiency. Today’s supply chains, however, are more complex and exposed to even more risk, requiring resilience, agility, and smarter decision‑making across warehousing and transportation operations. 

Pressure is constant, and from multiple directions, including geopolitical tensions, economic volatility, stringent government regulations, and rising customer expectations. The core challenge is no longer reacting to individual disruptions but overcoming constant firefighting. Leaders are seeking a clear path toward a more agile and future‑ready logistics strategy. Two questions sit at the center of this challenge: how fast do logistics operations have to change, and how complex are the operations that must be supported? 

Answering these questions starts with examining the technological foundations that power your current logistics operations. Long-standing solutions like SAP's ECC LE-WM, LE-TRA (ERP Central Component Logistics Execution Warehouse and Transportation Management), and Business Suite EWM/TM (Extended Warehouse Management/Transportation Management) have served reliably for decades, yet SAP continues to evolve to serve today's demands for real-time adaptability and scale.  

SAP’s logistics portfolio, which has supported customers across three decades, continues to evolve with adaptive, cloud-native, AI-driven platforms capable of learning, predicting, and orchestrating logistics processes autonomously, because that’s what companies need to compete now and in the future. 

The right journey depends on innovation speed, risk tolerance, and operational complexity.​ 

Future‑proofing logistics ultimately comes down to choosing the right modernization path. Every organization has a unique starting point, depending on how existing solutions like ECC LE-TRA/LE-WM or the SAP Business Suite have been implemented across the business. To choose the right next step, leaders need to understand the available options, determine what fits with the organization’s appetite for innovation, and assess the transformational impact on the team. 

Gartner’s logistics complexity model, breaking down process complexity across 5 levels, can help when setting a transformation strategy.  

Companies operating at levels 1 to 3 of Gartner’s model are typically characterized by manual or semi-automated processes, regional distribution, and lower complexity. These companies are best-suited for SaaS-native warehouse and/or transportation solutions that offer the implementation speed of standardized workflows, cloud qualities such as flexible, mobile interface, and advantages from improved scalability and lower TCO.  

Organizations at levels 4 and 5 require warehouse automation for high volume distribution, multi-modal transportation, and orchestration across multiple ERP systems. These companies seek the benefit of dedicated cloud environments that offer a higher level of adaptation for more control over their operations and their solution landscape. 

SAP offers different pathways to help businesses run and modernize across the span of operational complexity. Below are three journeys to guide your strategic planning for modernization. Each journey can be aligned to your high-level business ambitions, accounting for process complexity, innovation speed, and risk tolerance. 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Journey 1: Sustain Temporary Stability While You Prepare for Modernization 

Move your legacy ECC LE TRA/LE￼WM to S/4HANA Stockroom Management to keep your operations supported until 2040, giving you more time to manage your transformation. This journey is about sustaining what works until you need to evolve. It is designed for organizations that need more time to move their decades-old warehousing solution into the future. This approach, rooted in Stockroom Management, extends the life of a proven system without prematurely forcing change. It is a temporary but intentional holding pattern for conservative logistics strategies based on a firm foundation. Throughout this journey, SAP Logistics Management can be the go-to solution when you are ready to move from Stockroom Management. 

Journey 2a: Modernize Your Logistics in Controlled Steps   

For organizations that know modernization is essential but need to be mindful of organizational change management, this journey offers a stable path. It begins with moving on￼premise ECC LE-TRA/LE-WM to SAP S/4HANA Cloud, private edition, with basic Warehouse Management and Transportation Management capabilities, creating a modern foundation for future readiness. From there, companies can evolve their processes toward more advanced options as their needs grow (see 2b). This journey is best suited to businesses that want flexibility and control in their operations as well as in their change management. This journey also supports deeply integrated logistics that align with your RISE journey.  

Along this journey, if you find that your business complexity is manageable, SAP Logistics Management can even be incorporated as the most modern, AI-native SAP solution for logistics, to complement your ongoing operations.   

Journey 2b: Move Your large-scale Logistics to the cloud   

This journey supports organizations that need to manage highly specific processes, high-automation, and mission-critical logistics execution with one or multiple ERP systems. You can migrate from Business Suite EWM/TM to the cloud with S/4HANA Cloud Advanced EWM/TM without losing depth or control. This path can also serve as a second step for advancing operations from S/4HANA Cloud EWM/TM basic to S/4HANA Cloud EWM/TM advanced, to support your growing business needs.  

Journey 3: The Modernization Game-changer for Your Logistics  

For innovation leaders ready to sprint ahead of the competition, this journey represents the direct path to the most modern logistics operating model. Companies move from on-premise ECC LE-TRA/LE-WM to AI-native SAP Logistics Management bringing together cloud delivery, embedded intelligence, and network connectivity in a single solution for warehousing and logistics. This is the right path for businesses that want to standardize faster, simplify their landscape, and take advantage of continuous innovation. You gain a more connected and adaptive logistics model with built-in AI and a carrier network. Along this journey, you are setting the pace for modern supply chains.

The future of logistics is cloud-first, AI at the core, and modular by design. 

Logistics is accelerating into a new era, one defined not only by efficiency, but by intelligent adaptability and autonomous orchestration. SAP’s strategy, embodied in its evolving logistics portfolio, offers a clear, flexible, and future-ready path for companies at every level of complexity. 

Whether maintaining existing operations, maturing gradually as complexity increases, or leapfrogging to leading-edge capabilities, you can now compose and run your entire logistics operations on SAP. Basic to moderate complexity processes are supported with SAP Logistics Management and advanced and highly automated business with S/4HANA Cloud EWM and TM. By combining the two, you can design and achieve logistics networks to improve speed, agility, and resilience. 

Want to explore which logistics journey fits your business best? Don’t miss our Future of Supply Chain conversation with SAP’s Till Dengel: Logistics in the 21st Century: The Journey to the Cloud

entry.title: Driving the future with AI: Top Logistics Sessions at SAP Sapphire 2026
entry.content:
Better Starts Here.  Must see Logistics Sessions at SAP Sapphire 2026 in Orlando

Connect with business leaders on how they use SAP’s enterprise applications and AI to tackle their most pressing challenges at the SAP Sapphire & ASUG Annual Conference in Orlando. We are thrilled to share details about the Asset and Service Management presence at this three-day event in Orlando, Florida, from May 11-13.

Check out the list of Logistics sessions we’ve compiled that you won’t want to miss:

Monday, May 11:

 Unveiling SAP's logistics direction | SCM1726

Monday, May 11 | 3:30 PM - 3:50 PM EDT

Gain a strategic perspective on the evolution of logistics solutions from SAP through a road map outlining direction, innovations, and transformation approaches. Align your organization’s logistics transformation with SAP’s strategy to help ensure you’re positioned to meet long-term goals.

 

Tuesday, May 12

Next-generation supply chain | SCM2668

Tuesday, May 12 | 10:30 AM - 10:50 AM EDT

Explore how intelligent agents reshape supply chain management by enabling highly adaptive, agentic systems that evolve with constant change. These intelligent capabilities help organizations foresee disruptions, act instantly, and continuously optimize end‑to‑end operations.

AI‑enabled logistics: Scale faster, execute smarter | SCM1725

Tuesday, May 12 | 11:30 AM - 11:50 AM EDT

Explore AI innovations that elevate warehousing, transportation, and carrier collaboration with smarter planning, deeper insights, and more precise execution. Learn how AI-supported intelligence helps teams scale faster, boost efficiency, and build more agile, resilient logistics operations.

 AI‑enabled logistics: Scale faster, execute smarter | SCM1723

Tuesday, May 12 | 1:00 PM - 1:20 PM EDT

Explore AI innovations that elevate warehousing, transportation, and carrier collaboration with smarter planning, deeper insights, and more precise execution. Learn how AI-supported intelligence helps teams scale faster, boost efficiency, and build more agile, resilient logistics operations.

Extend procurement and supply chain collaboration with RISE with SAP | SCM2141

Tuesday, May 12 | 1:30 PM - 1:50 PM EDT

New customers on the RISE with SAP journey can accelerate value by activating their entitlement to SAP Business Network. Learn how connected procurement, supply chain, logistics, and asset management processes enhance collaboration, increase visibility, and drive resilient operations across your enterprise.

Dynamically adjusting supply chains: Orchestrate, adapt, and outperform | KEY2569

Tuesday, May 12 | 2:00 PM - 2:45 PM EDT

CEOs see supply chains as strategic assets—and COOs and chief supply chain officers must deliver. SAP software unites AI, contextual data, and apps to reimagine operations. From a user experience enhanced by the Joule solution to agent orchestration, transform your supply chain into a resilient, high-performance engine.

From strategy to value: AI-enabled supply chain transformation | SCM2861

Tuesday, May 12 | 3:00 PM - 3:45 PM EDT

SAP partner McKinsey & Company presents its Supply Chain Value Framework, followed by a customer discussion on SAP software adoption and AI-assisted innovation. Gain practical insights into turning strategy into real operational and financial value.

Standardization at scale: Schneider Electric’s journey to SAP Cloud ERP | ERP1765

Tuesday, May 12 | 3:30 PM - 3:50 PM EDT

Schneider Electric is executing an enterprise-wide transformation that consolidates multiple federated SAP software systems onto SAP Cloud ERP, simplifying the core, standardizing operations, and enabling consistent performance at scale. Hear about a standardized process template–driven approach spanning finance, manufacturing, and logistics.

 

Wednesday, May 13:

 Trailblazing distributed logistics: How Doehler is leading the way | SCM1724

Wednesday, May 13 | 10:30 AM - 10:50 AM EDT

Doehler GmbH is blazing a new path in coordinating warehouse and transportation across distributed sites. Learn how the ingredients producer unified operations, modernized carrier collaboration, and drove efficiency and performance across locations with the SAP Logistics Management solution.

Ferrara’s 99% standardized, AI-ready transformation on SAP S/4HANA | IND2307

Wednesday, May 13 | 11:00 AM - 11:20 AM EDT

Ferrara Candy Company’s move to SAP S/4HANA after a 25-module, big-bang go-live standardized 99% of planning, manufacturing, logistics, and finance processes using SAP Business Suite. Built on clean core principles, the greenfield transformation delivers more than 98% data quality and an AI-ready foundation for scalable growth and innovation.

 Be future-ready with innovations from SAP Business Network | SCM2142

Wednesday, May 13 | 1:00 PM - 1:45 PM EDT

Explore the future of SAP Business Network and how innovations will reshape collaboration across procurement, supply chain, logistics, and asset management. Learn about AI-assisted capabilities, expanded visibility, and suite-aligned workflows that elevate resilience, intelligence, and connectivity across your trading partner ecosystem.

 Modernizing global transportation: Illumina's path to delivery excellence | SCM1722

Wednesday, May 13 | 2:30 PM - 2:50 PM EDT

Hear from Illumina Inc. how life sciences logistics demands speed, precision, and resilience across global transportation operations. Learn how the biotech company tackled planning complexity and carrier coordination to improve transportation planning and execution using the SAP Transportation Management application and SAP Business Network.

AI-driven logistics orchestration | SCM2332

Demo station | Open During Show Floor Hours

Drive speed, resilience, and scale through AI‑orchestrated logistics by streamlining goods receipt, warehousing, distribution with carrier collaboration, and analytics. Learn how to connect remote sites, global hubs, and carriers to save cost, improve agility, and turn real‑time insights into AI-assisted execution across your supply chain.

Autonomous enterprise with SAP Cloud ERP Private | ERP2978

Demo station | Open During Show Floor Hours

Experience how SAP Cloud ERP Private supports the autonomous enterprise. See cross-domain processes—sales, procurement, manufacturing, logistics, assets, and service—transformed with intelligent automation. Explore how SAP Cloud ERP Private delivers agility, resilience, and control across your intelligent business core.

Start putting together your personal agenda today. Check out the full session catalog and stay updated on all the latest announcements by following us on LinkedIn SAP Supply Chain Management
